revision.comment: Auto generating page. date placed far in the past so it is attributed to Immanuelle and FireflyBot sends them messages if it is ever imported to Wikipedia.
revision.text: #redirect[[en:January (Lunar calendar)]] <noinclude>[[Category:New automatic wikipedia redirects]]</noinclude><includeonly>[[Category:Erroneous transclusions of January (Lunar calendar)]]</includeonly>

'''旧暦1月'''は、[[旧暦]]（[[太陰太陽暦]]）の年初から1番目の[[月 (暦)|月]]である。
[[天保暦]]よりも前の定義では、[[雨水]]を含む月を1月とする。[[新暦]]では[[1月]]下旬から[[3月]]上旬ごろに当たる。
1月の別名は'''睦月'''（'''むつき'''）である。名前の由来は[[1月]]を参照のこと。
1月30日は存在しない年もある
== 関連項目 == {{月 (暦)|旧暦1月|旧暦360日|月 (旧暦)|1|日 (旧暦)|[[1月30日 (旧暦)|30]]}}

1月の別名は'''睦月'''（'''むつき'''）である。名前の由来は[[1月]]を参照のこと。

revision.comment: 30日の存在についての記述を、[[5月 (旧暦)]]より転載
revision.text:
'''旧暦1月'''（きゅうれきいちがつ）は、[[旧暦]]（[[太陰太陽暦]]）の年初から1番目の[[月 (暦)|月]]である。
[[天保暦]]よりも前の定義では、[[雨水]]を含む月を1月とする。[[新暦]]では[[1月]]下旬から[[3月]]上旬ごろに当たる。
1月の別名は'''睦月'''（'''むつき'''）である。名前の由来は[[1月]]を参照のこと。
東洋の太陰太陽暦では月の日数である[[大小]]（大月30日、小月29日）が年により異なるため、1月29日までで1月30日は存在しない年もある。
== 関連項目 == *[[旧正月]] {{月 (暦)|旧暦1月|旧暦360日|月 (旧暦)|1|日 (旧暦)|[[1月30日 (旧暦)|30]]}}

revision.text:
{{Lunisolar January}} '''Lunisolar January''' (旧暦一月) is the first {{ill|Month (Calendar)|ja|月 (暦)|lt=Month|lt_ja=月}} of the year in the {{ill|Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|ja|旧暦|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|11=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar}} ({{ill|Lunisolar calendar|ja|太陰太陽暦}}).
Under definitions predating the {{ill|Tenpō Calendar|ja|天保暦}}, January was defined as the month that includes {{ill|Rain Water|ja|雨水}}. In the {{ill|New Calendar|ja|新暦|1=New Calendar (Japan)|lt=New Calendar}}, it corresponds approximately to late {{ill|January|ja|1月}} to early {{ill|March|ja|3月}}.
Another name for January is '''Mutsuki''' (睦月). For the origin of the name, see {{ill|January|ja|1月}}.
In the Eastern lunisolar calendar, the number of days in a month, known as {{ill|Large and Small|ja|大小}}, (with large months having 30 days and small months 29 days) varies from year to year. As a result, there are years when January ends on the 29th, with no January 30th.
== Dates in Mutsuki == {| class="wikitable" |+ Dates in Mutsuki ! Year !! New Moon !! Last Day !! Number of Days |- | 2014 || {{ill|January 31|ja|1月31日}} || {{ill|February 28|ja|2月28日}} || 29 days |- | 2015 || {{ill|February 19|ja|2月19日}} || {{ill|March 19|ja|3月19日}} || 29 days |- | 2016 || {{ill|February 8|ja|2月8日}} || {{ill|March 8|ja|3月8日}} || 30 days |- | 2017 || {{ill|January 28|ja|1月28日}} || {{ill|February 25|ja|2月25日}} || 29 days |- | 2018 || {{ill|February 16|ja|2月16日}} || {{ill|March 16|ja|3月16日}} || 29 days |- | 2019 || {{ill|February 5|ja|2月5日}} || {{ill|March 6|ja|3月6日}} || 30 days |- | 2020 || {{ill|January 25|ja|1月25日}} || {{ill|February 23|ja|2月23日}} || 30 days |- | 2021 || {{ill|February 12|ja|2月12日}} || {{ill|March 12|ja|3月12日}} || 29 days |- | 2022 || {{ill|February 1|ja|2月1日}} || {{ill|March 2|ja|3月2日}} || 30 days |- | 2023 || {{ill|January 22|ja|1月22日}} || {{ill|February 19|ja|2月19日}} || 29 days |- | 2024 || {{ill|February 10|ja|2月10日}} || {{ill|March 9|ja|3月9日}} || 29 days |- | 2025 || {{ill|January 29|ja|1月29日}} || {{ill|February 27|ja|2月27日}} || 30 days |}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Lunar New Year|ja|旧正月}} {{Month (Calendar)|Lunisolar January|List of Lunisolar Dates|Month (Lunisolar)|1|Day (Lunisolar)|{{ill|January 30 (Lunisolar)|ja|1月30日 (旧暦)|lt=30|lt_ja=30}}}}

{{citation needed}}
{{citation needed}} {{Japanese Traditional Lunisolar Months}}
[[cdo:Nùng-lĭk 1 nguŏk en]] [[gan:正月]] [[hak:Nùng-la̍k 1-ngie̍tja]]
[[wuu:正月]]
[[zh-min-nan:Kū-le̍k 1 goe̍h]] [[zh-yue:正月]]
[[cdo:Nùng-lĭk 1 nguŏk en]] [[gan:正月]] [[hak:Nùng-la̍k 1-ngie̍tja]]
[[wuu:正月]]
[[zh-min-nan:Kū-le̍k 1 goe̍h]] [[zh-yue:正月]]
{{Short description|First month of the year in the Chinese calendar}} {{unreferenced|date=January 2015}} {{Italic title|reason=[[:Category:Chinese words and phrases]]}} '''''Zhēngyuè''''' is the first [[month]] of the [[year]] in the [[Chinese calendar]]. In general, the Vernal Showers are in Zhēngyuè. The name comes from the annual [[morale]]-building [[Li (Confucian)|rite]] ({{zh|c=正旦大朝}}) in [[ancient China]].
Zhēngyuè 1 is the [[day]] of the [[rooster]]; Zhēngyuè 2 is the day of the [[dog]]; Zhēngyuè 3 is the day of the [[pig]]; Zhēngyuè 4 is the day of the [[goat]]; Zhēngyuè 5 is the day of the [[ox]]; Zhēngyuè 6 is the day of the [[horse]]; Zhēngyuè 7 is the day of the [[human]].
==Festivals== *[[Chinese New Year|New Year's Day]] ({{zh|c=春节}}) is Zhēngyuè 1. The following New Year's Day is January 29, 2025, which is a statutory [[holiday]]. *The [[Yuanxiao Festival]] ({{zh|c=元宵}}) is Zhēngyuè 15. The following Yuanxiao is February 12, 2025.
==Birth== *[[Wu Zetian]], the only [[female]] [[emperor]] of [[China]], Zhēngyuè 23, 624 *[[Li Bai]], a Chinese [[poet]] acclaimed to this day as a [[genius]] and romantic figure who took traditional [[poetry|poetic]] forms to new heights, Zhēngyuè 16, 701 *[[Emperor Shunzong of Tang]], an emperor of the Chinese [[Tang dynasty]], Zhēngyuè 12, 761 *[[Bai Juyi]], a Chinese [[poet]] of the Tang dynasty, Zhēngyuè 20, 772 *[[Emperor Xizong of Jin]], an emperor of the [[Jin dynasty (1115–1234)|Jin dynasty]] which controlled northern China from 1115 to 1234, Zhēngyuè 17, 1119 *[[Qiu Chuji]], a [[Daoist]] [[Wiktionary:disciple|disciple]] of [[Wang Chongyang]], Zhēngyuè 9, 1148 *[[Itō Suketaka]], a samurai, daimyō and twelfth family leader of the Itō clan, Zhēngyuè 15, 1559 *[[Li Hongzhang]], a [[politician]], [[general]], and [[diplomat]] of the late [[Qing Empire]], Zhēngyuè 5, 1823 *[[Aisin-Gioro Puyi]], the last emperor of China and the twelfth and final ruler of the [[Qing dynasty]], Zhēngyuè 14, 1906
==Death== *[[Sun Jian]], a military general and [[warlord]] who lived in the late [[Eastern Han dynasty]], Zhēngyuè 7, 191 *[[Guan Yu]], a general serving under the warlord [[Liu Bei]] in the late Eastern Han dynasty, Zhēngyuè 13, 220 *[[Cao Cao]], a warlord and the penultimate [[chancellor]] of the Eastern Han dynasty who rose to great power in the final years of the dynasty, Zhēngyuè 23, 220 *[[Emperor Xianzong of Tang]], an emperor of the Chinese Tang dynasty, Zhēngyuè 27, 820 *[[Emperor Yingzong of Song]], the fifth emperor of the [[Song dynasty]] of China, Zhēngyuè 7, 1067 *[[Emperor Zhezong of Song]], the seventh emperor of the Song dynasty of China, Zhēngyuè 12, 1100 *[[Kublai Khan]], the fifth [[Khagan]] (Great Khan) of the Ikh Mongol Uls ([[Mongol Empire]]), [[reign]]ing from 1260 to 1294, and the founder of the [[Yuan dynasty]] in [[Mongolia]] and China, Zhēngyuè 22, 1294 *[[Shunzhi Emperor]], the third emperor of the Qing dynasty and the first Qing emperor to rule over China, from 1644 to 1661, Zhēngyuè 30, 1638 *[[Qianlong Emperor]], the sixth emperor of the [[Manchu]]-led Qing dynasty, and the fourth Qing emperor to rule over China proper, Zhēngyuè 3, 1799 *[[Daoguang Emperor]], a Qing emperor, Zhēngyuè 14, 1850

Zhēngyuè 1 is the [[day]] of the [[rooster]]; Zhēngyuè 2 is the day of the [[dog]]; Zhēngyuè 3 is the day of the [[pig]]; Zhēngyuè 4 is the day of the [[goat]]; Zhēngyuè 5 is the day of the [[ox]]; Zhēngyuè 6 is the day of the [[horse]]; Zhēngyuè 7 is the day of the [[human]].
==Festivals== *[[Chinese New Year|New Year's Day]] ({{zh|c=春节}}) is Zhēngyuè 1. The following New Year's Day is January 29, 2025, which is a statutory [[holiday]]. *The [[Yuanxiao Festival]] ({{zh|c=元宵}}) is Zhēngyuè 15. The following Yuanxiao is February 12, 2025.
==Birth== *[[Wu Zetian]], the only [[female]] [[emperor]] of [[China]], Zhēngyuè 23, 624 *[[Li Bai]], a Chinese [[poet]] acclaimed to this day as a [[genius]] and romantic figure who took traditional [[poetry|poetic]] forms to new heights, Zhēngyuè 16, 701 *[[Emperor Shunzong of Tang]], an emperor of the Chinese [[Tang dynasty]], Zhēngyuè 12, 761 *[[Bai Juyi]], a Chinese [[poet]] of the Tang dynasty, Zhēngyuè 20, 772 *[[Emperor Xizong of Jin]], an emperor of the [[Jin dynasty (1115–1234)|Jin dynasty]] which controlled northern China from 1115 to 1234, Zhēngyuè 17, 1119 *[[Qiu Chuji]], a [[Daoist]] [[Wiktionary:disciple|disciple]] of [[Wang Chongyang]], Zhēngyuè 9, 1148 *[[Itō Suketaka]], a samurai, daimyō and twelfth family leader of the Itō clan, Zhēngyuè 15, 1559 *[[Li Hongzhang]], a [[politician]], [[general]], and [[diplomat]] of the late [[Qing Empire]], Zhēngyuè 5, 1823 *[[Aisin-Gioro Puyi]], the last emperor of China and the twelfth and final ruler of the [[Qing dynasty]], Zhēngyuè 14, 1906
==Death== *[[Sun Jian]], a military general and [[warlord]] who lived in the late [[Eastern Han dynasty]], Zhēngyuè 7, 191 *[[Guan Yu]], a general serving under the warlord [[Liu Bei]] in the late Eastern Han dynasty, Zhēngyuè 13, 220 *[[Cao Cao]], a warlord and the penultimate [[chancellor]] of the Eastern Han dynasty who rose to great power in the final years of the dynasty, Zhēngyuè 23, 220 *[[Emperor Xianzong of Tang]], an emperor of the Chinese Tang dynasty, Zhēngyuè 27, 820 *[[Emperor Yingzong of Song]], the fifth emperor of the [[Song dynasty]] of China, Zhēngyuè 7, 1067 *[[Emperor Zhezong of Song]], the seventh emperor of the Song dynasty of China, Zhēngyuè 12, 1100 *[[Kublai Khan]], the fifth [[Khagan]] (Great Khan) of the Ikh Mongol Uls ([[Mongol Empire]]), [[reign]]ing from 1260 to 1294, and the founder of the [[Yuan dynasty]] in [[Mongolia]] and China, Zhēngyuè 22, 1294 *[[Shunzhi Emperor]], the third emperor of the Qing dynasty and the first Qing emperor to rule over China, from 1644 to 1661, Zhēngyuè 30, 1638 *[[Qianlong Emperor]], the sixth emperor of the [[Manchu]]-led Qing dynasty, and the fourth Qing emperor to rule over China proper, Zhēngyuè 3, 1799 *[[Daoguang Emperor]], a Qing emperor, Zhēngyuè 14, 1850

{{Lunisolar January}} '''Lunisolar January''' (旧暦一月) is the first {{ill|Month (Calendar)|ja|月 (暦)|lt=Month|lt_ja=月|12=draft}} of the year in the {{ill|Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|ja|旧暦|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|11=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|12=draft}} ({{ill|Lunisolar calendar|ja|太陰太陽暦|12=draft}}).
Under definitions predating the {{ill|Tenpō Calendar|ja|天保暦|12=draft}}, January was defined as the month that includes {{ill|Rain Water|ja|雨水|12=draft}}. In the {{ill|New Calendar|ja|新暦|1=New Calendar (Japan)|lt=New Calendar|12=draft}}, it corresponds approximately to late {{ill|January|ja|1月|12=draft}} to early {{ill|March|ja|3月|12=draft}}.
Another name for January is '''Mutsuki''' (睦月). For the origin of the name, see {{ill|January|ja|1月|12=draft}}.
In the Eastern lunisolar calendar, the number of days in a month, known as {{ill|Large and Small|ja|大小|12=draft}}, (with large months having 30 days and small months 29 days) varies from year to year. As a result, there are years when January ends on the 29th, with no January 30th.
== Dates in Mutsuki == {| class="wikitable" |+ Dates in Mutsuki ! Year !! New Moon !! Last Day !! {{Short description|First month of the year in the Chinese calendar}} {{unreferenced|date=January 2015}} {{Italic title|reason=[[:Category:Chinese words and phrases]]}} '''''Zhēngyuè''''' is the first [[month]] of the [[year]] in the [[Chinese calendar]]. In general, the Vernal Showers are in Zhēngyuè. The name comes from the annual [[morale]]-building [[Li (Confucian)|rite]] ({{zh|c=正旦大朝}}) in [[ancient China]].
Zhēngyuè 1 is the [[day]] of the [[rooster]]; Zhēngyuè 2 is the day of the [[dog]]; Zhēngyuè 3 is the day of the [[pig]]; Zhēngyuè 4 is the day of the [[goat]]; Zhēngyuè 5 is the day of the [[ox]]; Zhēngyuè 6 is the day of the [[horse]]; Zhēngyuè 7 is the day of the [[human]].
==Festivals== *[[Chinese New Year|New Year's Day]] ({{zh|c=春节}}) is Zhēngyuè 1. The following New Year's Day is January 29, 2025, which is a statutory [[holiday]]. *The [[Yuanxiao Festival]] ({{zh|c=元宵}}) is Zhēngyuè 15. The following Yuanxiao is February 12, 2025.
==Birth== *[[Wu Zetian]], the only [[female]] [[emperor]] of [[China]], Zhēngyuè 23, 624 *[[Li Bai]], a Chinese [[poet]] acclaimed to this day as a [[genius]] and romantic figure who took traditional [[poetry|poetic]] forms to new heights, Zhēngyuè 16, 701 *[[Emperor Shunzong of Tang]], an emperor of the Chinese [[Tang dynasty]], Zhēngyuè 12, 761 *[[Bai Juyi]], a Chinese [[poet]] of the Tang dynasty, Zhēngyuè 20, 772 *[[Emperor Xizong of Jin]], an emperor of the [[Jin dynasty (1115–1234)|Jin dynasty]] which controlled northern China from 1115 to 1234, Zhēngyuè 17, 1119 *[[Qiu Chuji]], a [[Daoist]] [[Wiktionary:disciple|disciple]] of [[Wang Chongyang]], Zhēngyuè 9, 1148 *[[Itō Suketaka]], a samurai, daimyō and twelfth family leader of the Itō clan, Zhēngyuè 15, 1559 *[[Li Hongzhang]], a [[politician]], [[general]], and [[diplomat]] of the late [[Qing Empire]], Zhēngyuè 5, 1823 *[[Aisin-Gioro Puyi]], the last emperor of China and the twelfth and final ruler of the [[Qing dynasty]], Zhēngyuè 14, 1906
==Death== *[[Sun Jian]], a military general and [[warlord]] who lived in the late [[Eastern Han dynasty]], Zhēngyuè 7, 191 *[[Guan Yu]], a general serving under the warlord [[Liu Bei]] in the late Eastern Han dynasty, Zhēngyuè 13, 220 *[[Cao Cao]], a warlord and the penultimate [[chancellor]] of the Eastern Han dynasty who rose to great power in the final years of the dynasty, Zhēngyuè 23, 220 *[[Emperor Xianzong of Tang]], an emperor of the Chinese Tang dynasty, Zhēngyuè 27, 820 *[[Emperor Yingzong of Song]], the fifth emperor of the [[Song dynasty]] of China, Zhēngyuè 7, 1067 *[[Emperor Zhezong of Song]], the seventh emperor of the Song dynasty of China, Zhēngyuè 12, 1100 *[[Kublai Khan]], the fifth [[Khagan]] (Great Khan) of the Ikh Mongol Uls ([[Mongol Empire]]), [[reign]]ing from 1260 to 1294, and the founder of the [[Yuan dynasty]] in [[Mongolia]] and China, Zhēngyuè 22, 1294 *[[Shunzhi Emperor]], the third emperor of the Qing dynasty and the first Qing emperor to rule over China, from 1644 to 1661, Zhēngyuè 30, 1638 *[[Qianlong Emperor]], the sixth emperor of the [[Manchu]]-led Qing dynasty, and the fourth Qing emperor to rule over China proper, Zhēngyuè 3, 1799 *[[Daoguang Emperor]], a Qing emperor, Zhēngyuè 14, 1850

{{Lunisolar January}} '''Lunisolar January''' (旧暦一月) is the first {{ill|Month (Calendar)|ja|月 (暦)|lt=Month|lt_ja=月|12=draft|12=draft}} of the year in the {{ill|Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|ja|旧暦|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|11=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|12=draft|12=draft}} ({{ill|Lunisolar calendar|ja|太陰太陽暦|12=draft|12=draft}}).
Under definitions predating the {{ill|Tenpō Calendar|ja|天保暦|12=draft|12=draft}}, January was defined as the month that includes {{ill|Rain Water|ja|雨水|12=draft|12=draft}}. In the {{ill|New Calendar|ja|新暦|1=New Calendar (Japan)|lt=New Calendar|12=draft|12=draft}}, it corresponds approximately to late {{ill|January|ja|1月|12=draft|12=draft}} to early {{ill|March|ja|3月|12=draft|12=draft}}.
Another name for January is '''Mutsuki''' (睦月). For the origin of the name, see {{ill|January|ja|1月|12=draft|12=draft}}.
In the Eastern lunisolar calendar, the number of days in a month, known as {{ill|Large and Small|ja|大小|12=draft|12=draft}}, (with large months having 30 days and small months 29 days) varies from year to year. As a result, there are years when January ends on the 29th, with no January 30th.

In general, the Vernal Showers are in Zhēngyuè. The name comes from the annual [[morale]]-building [[Li (Confucian)|rite]] ({{zh|c=正旦大朝}}) in [[ancient China]].
Zhēngyuè 1 is the [[day]] of the [[rooster]]; Zhēngyuè 2 is the day of the [[dog]]; Zhēngyuè 3 is the day of the [[pig]]; Zhēngyuè 4 is the day of the [[goat]]; Zhēngyuè 5 is the day of the [[ox]]; Zhēngyuè 6 is the day of the [[horse]]; Zhēngyuè 7 is the day of the [[human]].
==Festivals== *[[Chinese New Year|New Year's Day]] ({{zh|c=春节}}) is Zhēngyuè 1. The following New Year's Day is January 29, 2025, which is a statutory [[holiday]]. *The [[Yuanxiao Festival]] ({{zh|c=元宵}}) is Zhēngyuè 15. The following Yuanxiao is February 12, 2025.
==Birth== *[[Wu Zetian]], the only [[female]] [[emperor]] of [[China]], Zhēngyuè 23, 624 *[[Li Bai]], a Chinese [[poet]] acclaimed to this day as a [[genius]] and romantic figure who took traditional [[poetry|poetic]] forms to new heights, Zhēngyuè 16, 701 *[[Emperor Shunzong of Tang]], an emperor of the Chinese [[Tang dynasty]], Zhēngyuè 12, 761 *[[Bai Juyi]], a Chinese [[poet]] of the Tang dynasty, Zhēngyuè 20, 772 *[[Emperor Xizong of Jin]], an emperor of the [[Jin dynasty (1115–1234)|Jin dynasty]] which controlled northern China from 1115 to 1234, Zhēngyuè 17, 1119 *[[Qiu Chuji]], a [[Daoist]] [[Wiktionary:disciple|disciple]] of [[Wang Chongyang]], Zhēngyuè 9, 1148 *[[Itō Suketaka]], a samurai, daimyō and twelfth family leader of the Itō clan, Zhēngyuè 15, 1559 *[[Li Hongzhang]], a [[politician]], [[general]], and [[diplomat]] of the late [[Qing Empire]], Zhēngyuè 5, 1823 *[[Aisin-Gioro Puyi]], the last emperor of China and the twelfth and final ruler of the [[Qing dynasty]], Zhēngyuè 14, 1906
==Death== *[[Sun Jian]], a military general and [[warlord]] who lived in the late [[Eastern Han dynasty]], Zhēngyuè 7, 191 *[[Guan Yu]], a general serving under the warlord [[Liu Bei]] in the late Eastern Han dynasty, Zhēngyuè 13, 220 *[[Cao Cao]], a warlord and the penultimate [[chancellor]] of the Eastern Han dynasty who rose to great power in the final years of the dynasty, Zhēngyuè 23, 220 *[[Emperor Xianzong of Tang]], an emperor of the Chinese Tang dynasty, Zhēngyuè 27, 820 *[[Emperor Yingzong of Song]], the fifth emperor of the [[Song dynasty]] of China, Zhēngyuè 7, 1067 *[[Emperor Zhezong of Song]], the seventh emperor of the Song dynasty of China, Zhēngyuè 12, 1100 *[[Kublai Khan]], the fifth [[Khagan]] (Great Khan) of the Ikh Mongol Uls ([[Mongol Empire]]), [[reign]]ing from 1260 to 1294, and the founder of the [[Yuan dynasty]] in [[Mongolia]] and China, Zhēngyuè 22, 1294 *[[Shunzhi Emperor]], the third emperor of the Qing dynasty and the first Qing emperor to rule over China, from 1644 to 1661, Zhēngyuè 30, 1638 *[[Qianlong Emperor]], the sixth emperor of the [[Manchu]]-led Qing dynasty, and the fourth Qing emperor to rule over China proper, Zhēngyuè 3, 1799 *[[Daoguang Emperor]], a Qing emperor, Zhēngyuè 14, 1850

{{Lunisolar January}} '''Lunisolar January''' (旧暦一月) is the first {{ill|Month (Calendar)|ja|月 (暦)|lt=Month|lt_ja=月|12=draft|12=draft}} of the year in the {{ill|Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|ja|旧暦|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|11=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|12=draft|12=draft}} ({{ill|Lunisolar calendar|ja|太陰太陽暦|12=draft|12=draft}}).
Under definitions predating the {{ill|Tenpō Calendar|ja|天保暦|12=draft|12=draft}}, January was defined as the month that includes {{ill|Rain Water|ja|雨水|12=draft|12=draft}}. In the {{ill|New Calendar|ja|新暦|1=New Calendar (Japan)|lt=New Calendar|12=draft|12=draft}}, it corresponds approximately to late {{ill|January|ja|1月|12=draft|12=draft}} to early {{ill|March|ja|3月|12=draft|12=draft}}.
Another name for January is '''Mutsuki''' (睦月). For the origin of the name, see {{ill|January|ja|1月|12=draft|12=draft}}.
In the Eastern lunisolar calendar, the number of days in a month, known as {{ill|Large and Small|ja|大小|12=draft|12=draft}}, (with large months having 30 days and small months 29 days) varies from year to year. As a result, there are years when January ends on the 29th, with no January 30th.

{{Short description|First month of the year in the Chinese calendar}} {{unreferenced|date=January 2015}} {{Italic title|reason=[[:Category:Chinese words and phrases]]}} '''''Zhēngyuè''''' is the first [[month]] of the [[year]] in the [[Chinese calendar]]. In general, the Vernal Showers are in Zhēngyuè. The name comes from the annual [[morale]]-building [[Li (Confucian)|rite]] ({{zh|c=正旦大朝}}) in [[ancient China]].
Zhēngyuè 1 is the [[day]] of the [[rooster]]; Zhēngyuè 2 is the day of the [[dog]]; Zhēngyuè 3 is the day of the [[pig]]; Zhēngyuè 4 is the day of the [[goat]]; Zhēngyuè 5 is the day of the [[ox]]; Zhēngyuè 6 is the day of the [[horse]]; Zhēngyuè 7 is the day of the [[human]].
==Festivals== *[[Chinese New Year|New Year's Day]] ({{zh|c=春节}}) is Zhēngyuè 1. The following New Year's Day is January 29, 2025, which is a statutory [[holiday]]. *The [[Yuanxiao Festival]] ({{zh|c=元宵}}) is Zhēngyuè 15. The following Yuanxiao is February 12, 2025.
==Birth== *[[Wu Zetian]], the only [[female]] [[emperor]] of [[China]], Zhēngyuè 23, 624 *[[Li Bai]], a Chinese [[poet]] acclaimed to this day as a [[genius]] and romantic figure who took traditional [[poetry|poetic]] forms to new heights, Zhēngyuè 16, 701 *[[Emperor Shunzong of Tang]], an emperor of the Chinese [[Tang dynasty]], Zhēngyuè 12, 761 *[[Bai Juyi]], a Chinese [[poet]] of the Tang dynasty, Zhēngyuè 20, 772 *[[Emperor Xizong of Jin]], an emperor of the [[Jin dynasty (1115–1234)|Jin dynasty]] which controlled northern China from 1115 to 1234, Zhēngyuè 17, 1119 *[[Qiu Chuji]], a [[Daoist]] [[Wiktionary:disciple|disciple]] of [[Wang Chongyang]], Zhēngyuè 9, 1148 *[[Itō Suketaka]], a samurai, daimyō and twelfth family leader of the Itō clan, Zhēngyuè 15, 1559 *[[Li Hongzhang]], a [[politician]], [[general]], and [[diplomat]] of the late [[Qing Empire]], Zhēngyuè 5, 1823 *[[Aisin-Gioro Puyi]], the last emperor of China and the twelfth and final ruler of the [[Qing dynasty]], Zhēngyuè 14, 1906
==Death== *[[Sun Jian]], a military general and [[warlord]] who lived in the late [[Eastern Han dynasty]], Zhēngyuè 7, 191 *[[Guan Yu]], a general serving under the warlord [[Liu Bei]] in the late Eastern Han dynasty, Zhēngyuè 13, 220 *[[Cao Cao]], a warlord and the penultimate [[chancellor]] of the Eastern Han dynasty who rose to great power in the final years of the dynasty, Zhēngyuè 23, 220 *[[Emperor Xianzong of Tang]], an emperor of the Chinese Tang dynasty, Zhēngyuè 27, 820 *[[Emperor Yingzong of Song]], the fifth emperor of the [[Song dynasty]] of China, Zhēngyuè 7, 1067 *[[Emperor Zhezong of Song]], the seventh emperor of the Song dynasty of China, Zhēngyuè 12, 1100 *[[Kublai Khan]], the fifth [[Khagan]] (Great Khan) of the Ikh Mongol Uls ([[Mongol Empire]]), [[reign]]ing from 1260 to 1294, and the founder of the [[Yuan dynasty]] in [[Mongolia]] and China, Zhēngyuè 22, 1294 *[[Shunzhi Emperor]], the third emperor of the Qing dynasty and the first Qing emperor to rule over China, from 1644 to 1661, Zhēngyuè 30, 1638 *[[Qianlong Emperor]], the sixth emperor of the [[Manchu]]-led Qing dynasty, and the fourth Qing emperor to rule over China proper, Zhēngyuè 3, 1799 *[[Daoguang Emperor]], a Qing emperor, Zhēngyuè 14, 1850 {{draft categories| [[Category:Lunisolar January]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Pages that attribution was given to on Feb 22]] [[Category:Expanded pages from wikipedia]] [[Category:Chinese calendars]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Lunisolar months]] [[Category:WikiProject Japanese Calendar]] [[Category:Lunisolar]] [[Category:I think I cleared all the incomplete or blank pages earlier]] }} [[en:Zhengyue]] [[sn:旧暦一月]] [[sn:Zhengyue]] [[en:Lunisolar January]] [[sn:January (Lunar calendar)]] [[sn:1月 (旧暦)]] [[ko:음력 1월]] [[th:เดือนอ้าย]] [[vi:Tháng giêng]] [[za:Ndwencieng]] [[zh:正月]] [[ja:1月 (旧暦)]] [[sn:hengyue]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Zhengyue}}

{{Lunisolar January}} '''Lunisolar January''' (旧暦一月) is the first {{ill|Month (Calendar)|ja|月 (暦)|lt=Month|lt_ja=月|12=draft|12=draft}} of the year in the {{ill|Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|ja|旧暦|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|11=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|12=draft|12=draft}} ({{ill|Lunisolar calendar|ja|太陰太陽暦|12=draft|12=draft}}).
Under definitions predating the {{ill|Tenpō Calendar|ja|天保暦|12=draft|12=draft}}, January was defined as the month that includes {{ill|Rain Water|ja|雨水|12=draft|12=draft}}. In the {{ill|New Calendar|ja|新暦|1=New Calendar (Japan)|lt=New Calendar|12=draft|12=draft}}, it corresponds approximately to late {{ill|January|ja|1月|12=draft|12=draft}} to early {{ill|March|ja|3月|12=draft|12=draft}}.
Another name for January is '''Mutsuki''' (睦月). For the origin of the name, see {{ill|January|ja|1月|12=draft|12=draft}}.
In the Eastern lunisolar calendar, the number of days in a month, known as {{ill|Large and Small|ja|大小|12=draft|12=draft}}, (with large months having 30 days and small months 29 days) varies from year to year. As a result, there are years when January ends on the 29th, with no January 30th.
== Dates in Mutsuki == {| class="wikitable" |+ Dates in Mutsuki ! Year !! New Moon !! Last Day !! {{Short description|First month of the year in the Chinese calendar}} {{unreferenced|date=January 2015}} {{Italic title|reason=[[:Category:Chinese words and phrases]]}} '''''Zhēngyuè''''' is the first [[month]] of the [[year]] in the [[Chinese calendar]]. In general, the Vernal Showers are in Zhēngyuè. The name comes from the annual [[morale]]-building [[Li (Confucian)|rite]] ({{zh|c=正旦大朝}}) in [[ancient China]].
Zhēngyuè 1 is the [[day]] of the [[rooster]]; Zhēngyuè 2 is the day of the [[dog]]; Zhēngyuè 3 is the day of the [[pig]]; Zhēngyuè 4 is the day of the [[goat]]; Zhēngyuè 5 is the day of the [[ox]]; Zhēngyuè 6 is the day of the [[horse]]; Zhēngyuè 7 is the day of the [[human]].
==Festivals== *[[Chinese New Year|New Year's Day]] ({{zh|c=春节}}) is Zhēngyuè 1. The following New Year's Day is January 29, 2025, which is a statutory [[holiday]]. *The [[Yuanxiao Festival]] ({{zh|c=元宵}}) is Zhēngyuè 15. The following Yuanxiao is February 12, 2025.
==Birth== *[[Wu Zetian]], the only [[female]] [[emperor]] of [[China]], Zhēngyuè 23, 624 *[[Li Bai]], a Chinese [[poet]] acclaimed to this day as a [[genius]] and romantic figure who took traditional [[poetry|poetic]] forms to new heights, Zhēngyuè 16, 701 *[[Emperor Shunzong of Tang]], an emperor of the Chinese [[Tang dynasty]], Zhēngyuè 12, 761 *[[Bai Juyi]], a Chinese [[poet]] of the Tang dynasty, Zhēngyuè 20, 772 *[[Emperor Xizong of Jin]], an emperor of the [[Jin dynasty (1115–1234)|Jin dynasty]] which controlled northern China from 1115 to 1234, Zhēngyuè 17, 1119 *[[Qiu Chuji]], a [[Daoist]] [[Wiktionary:disciple|disciple]] of [[Wang Chongyang]], Zhēngyuè 9, 1148 *[[Itō Suketaka]], a samurai, daimyō and twelfth family leader of the Itō clan, Zhēngyuè 15, 1559 *[[Li Hongzhang]], a [[politician]], [[general]], and [[diplomat]] of the late [[Qing Empire]], Zhēngyuè 5, 1823 *[[Aisin-Gioro Puyi]], the last emperor of China and the twelfth and final ruler of the [[Qing dynasty]], Zhēngyuè 14, 1906
==Death== *[[Sun Jian]], a military general and [[warlord]] who lived in the late [[Eastern Han dynasty]], Zhēngyuè 7, 191 *[[Guan Yu]], a general serving under the warlord [[Liu Bei]] in the late Eastern Han dynasty, Zhēngyuè 13, 220 *[[Cao Cao]], a warlord and the penultimate [[chancellor]] of the Eastern Han dynasty who rose to great power in the final years of the dynasty, Zhēngyuè 23, 220 *[[Emperor Xianzong of Tang]], an emperor of the Chinese Tang dynasty, Zhēngyuè 27, 820 *[[Emperor Yingzong of Song]], the fifth emperor of the [[Song dynasty]] of China, Zhēngyuè 7, 1067 *[[Emperor Zhezong of Song]], the seventh emperor of the Song dynasty of China, Zhēngyuè 12, 1100 *[[Kublai Khan]], the fifth [[Khagan]] (Great Khan) of the Ikh Mongol Uls ([[Mongol Empire]]), [[reign]]ing from 1260 to 1294, and the founder of the [[Yuan dynasty]] in [[Mongolia]] and China, Zhēngyuè 22, 1294 *[[Shunzhi Emperor]], the third emperor of the Qing dynasty and the first Qing emperor to rule over China, from 1644 to 1661, Zhēngyuè 30, 1638 *[[Qianlong Emperor]], the sixth emperor of the [[Manchu]]-led Qing dynasty, and the fourth Qing emperor to rule over China proper, Zhēngyuè 3, 1799 *[[Daoguang Emperor]], a Qing emperor, Zhēngyuè 14, 1850 {{draft categories| [[Category:Lunisolar January]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Pages that attribution was given to on Feb 22]] [[Category:Expanded pages from wikipedia]] [[Category:Chinese calendars]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Lunisolar months]] [[Category:WikiProject Japanese Calendar]] [[Category:Lunisolar]] }} [[en:Zhengyue]] [[sn:旧暦一月]] [[sn:Zhengyue]] [[en:Lunisolar January]] [[sn:January (Lunar calendar)]] [[sn:1月 (旧暦)]] [[ko:음력 1월]] [[th:เดือนอ้าย]] [[vi:Tháng giêng]] [[za:Ndwencieng]] [[zh:正月]] [[ja:1月 (旧暦)]] [[sn:hengyue]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Zhengyue}}

{{Lunisolar January}} '''Lunisolar January''' (旧暦一月) is the first {{ill|Month (Calendar)|ja|月 (暦)|lt=Month|lt_ja=月|12=draft|12=draft}} of the year in the {{ill|Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|ja|旧暦|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|11=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|12=draft|12=draft}} ({{ill|Lunisolar calendar|ja|太陰太陽暦|12=draft|12=draft}}).
Under definitions predating the {{ill|Tenpō Calendar|ja|天保暦|12=draft|12=draft}}, January was defined as the month that includes {{ill|Rain Water|ja|雨水|12=draft|12=draft}}. In the {{ill|New Calendar|ja|新暦|1=New Calendar (Japan)|lt=New Calendar|12=draft|12=draft}}, it corresponds approximately to late {{ill|January|ja|1月|12=draft|12=draft}} to early {{ill|March|ja|3月|12=draft|12=draft}}.
Another name for January is '''Mutsuki''' (睦月). For the origin of the name, see {{ill|January|ja|1月|12=draft|12=draft}}.
In the Eastern lunisolar calendar, the number of days in a month, known as {{ill|Large and Small|ja|大小|12=draft|12=draft}}, (with large months having 30 days and small months 29 days) varies from year to year. As a result, there are years when January ends on the 29th, with no January 30th.

{{Short description|First month of the year in the Chinese calendar}} {{unreferenced|date=January 2015}} {{Italic title|reason=[[:Category:Chinese words and phrases]]}} '''''Zhēngyuè''''' is the first [[month]] of the [[year]] in the [[Chinese calendar]]. In general, the Vernal Showers are in Zhēngyuè. The name comes from the annual [[morale]]-building [[Li (Confucian)|rite]] ({{zh|c=正旦大朝}}) in [[ancient China]].
Zhēngyuè 1 is the [[day]] of the [[rooster]]; Zhēngyuè 2 is the day of the [[dog]]; Zhēngyuè 3 is the day of the [[pig]]; Zhēngyuè 4 is the day of the [[goat]]; Zhēngyuè 5 is the day of the [[ox]]; Zhēngyuè 6 is the day of the [[horse]]; Zhēngyuè 7 is the day of the [[human]].
==Festivals== *[[Chinese New Year|New Year's Day]] ({{zh|c=春节}}) is Zhēngyuè 1. The following New Year's Day is January 29, 2025, which is a statutory [[holiday]]. *The [[Yuanxiao Festival]] ({{zh|c=元宵}}) is Zhēngyuè 15. The following Yuanxiao is February 12, 2025.
==Birth== *[[Wu Zetian]], the only [[female]] [[emperor]] of [[China]], Zhēngyuè 23, 624 *[[Li Bai]], a Chinese [[poet]] acclaimed to this day as a [[genius]] and romantic figure who took traditional [[poetry|poetic]] forms to new heights, Zhēngyuè 16, 701 *[[Emperor Shunzong of Tang]], an emperor of the Chinese [[Tang dynasty]], Zhēngyuè 12, 761 *[[Bai Juyi]], a Chinese [[poet]] of the Tang dynasty, Zhēngyuè 20, 772 *[[Emperor Xizong of Jin]], an emperor of the [[Jin dynasty (1115–1234)|Jin dynasty]] which controlled northern China from 1115 to 1234, Zhēngyuè 17, 1119 *[[Qiu Chuji]], a [[Daoist]] [[Wiktionary:disciple|disciple]] of [[Wang Chongyang]], Zhēngyuè 9, 1148 *[[Itō Suketaka]], a samurai, daimyō and twelfth family leader of the Itō clan, Zhēngyuè 15, 1559 *[[Li Hongzhang]], a [[politician]], [[general]], and [[diplomat]] of the late [[Qing Empire]], Zhēngyuè 5, 1823 *[[Aisin-Gioro Puyi]], the last emperor of China and the twelfth and final ruler of the [[Qing dynasty]], Zhēngyuè 14, 1906
==Death== *[[Sun Jian]], a military general and [[warlord]] who lived in the late [[Eastern Han dynasty]], Zhēngyuè 7, 191 *[[Guan Yu]], a general serving under the warlord [[Liu Bei]] in the late Eastern Han dynasty, Zhēngyuè 13, 220 *[[Cao Cao]], a warlord and the penultimate [[chancellor]] of the Eastern Han dynasty who rose to great power in the final years of the dynasty, Zhēngyuè 23, 220 *[[Emperor Xianzong of Tang]], an emperor of the Chinese Tang dynasty, Zhēngyuè 27, 820 *[[Emperor Yingzong of Song]], the fifth emperor of the [[Song dynasty]] of China, Zhēngyuè 7, 1067 *[[Emperor Zhezong of Song]], the seventh emperor of the Song dynasty of China, Zhēngyuè 12, 1100 *[[Kublai Khan]], the fifth [[Khagan]] (Great Khan) of the Ikh Mongol Uls ([[Mongol Empire]]), [[reign]]ing from 1260 to 1294, and the founder of the [[Yuan dynasty]] in [[Mongolia]] and China, Zhēngyuè 22, 1294 *[[Shunzhi Emperor]], the third emperor of the Qing dynasty and the first Qing emperor to rule over China, from 1644 to 1661, Zhēngyuè 30, 1638 *[[Qianlong Emperor]], the sixth emperor of the [[Manchu]]-led Qing dynasty, and the fourth Qing emperor to rule over China proper, Zhēngyuè 3, 1799 *[[Daoguang Emperor]], a Qing emperor, Zhēngyuè 14, 1850 {{draft categories| [[Category:Lunisolar January]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:Pages that attribution was given to on Feb 22]] [[Category:Expanded pages from wikipedia]] [[Category:Chinese calendars]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Lunisolar months]] [[Category:WikiProject Japanese Calendar]] [[Category:Lunisolar]] }} [[en:Zhengyue]] [[sn:旧暦一月]] [[sn:Zhengyue]] [[en:Lunisolar January]] [[sn:January (Lunar calendar)]] [[sn:1月 (旧暦)]] [[ko:음력 1월]] [[th:เดือนอ้าย]] [[vi:Tháng giêng]] [[za:Ndwencieng]] [[zh:正月]] [[ja:1月 (旧暦)]] [[sn:hengyue]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Zhengyue}} [[ar:Zhengyue]]

{{Lunisolar January}} '''Lunisolar January''' (旧暦一月) is the first {{ill|Month (Calendar)|ja|月 (暦)|lt=Month|lt_ja=月|12=draft|12=draft}} of the year in the {{ill|Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|ja|旧暦|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|11=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|12=draft|12=draft}} ({{ill|Lunisolar calendar|ja|太陰太陽暦|12=draft|12=draft}}).
Under definitions predating the {{ill|Tenpō Calendar|ja|天保暦|12=draft|12=draft}}, January was defined as the month that includes {{ill|Rain Water|ja|雨水|12=draft|12=draft}}. In the {{ill|New Calendar|ja|新暦|1=New Calendar (Japan)|lt=New Calendar|12=draft|12=draft}}, it corresponds approximately to late {{ill|January|ja|1月|12=draft|12=draft}} to early {{ill|March|ja|3月|12=draft|12=draft}}.
Another name for January is '''Mutsuki''' (睦月). For the origin of the name, see {{ill|January|ja|1月|12=draft|12=draft}}.
In the Eastern lunisolar calendar, the number of days in a month, known as {{ill|Large and Small|ja|大小|12=draft|12=draft}}, (with large months having 30 days and small months 29 days) varies from year to year. As a result, there are years when January ends on the 29th, with no January 30th.
== Dates in Mutsuki == {| class="wikitable" |+ Dates in Mutsuki ! Year !! New Moon !! Last Day !! revision.text:
{{Lunisolar January|qq=}} ''''Zhēngyuè''''' is the first [[month]] of the [[year]] in the [[Chinese calendar]]. In general, the Vernal Showers are in Zhēngyuè. The name comes from the annual [[morale]]-building [[Li (Confucian)|rite]] ({{zh|c=正旦大朝|qq=}}) in [[ancient China]].
Zhēngyuè 1 is the [[day]] of the [[rooster]]; Zhēngyuè 2 is the day of the [[dog]]; Zhēngyuè 3 is the day of the [[pig]]; Zhēngyuè 4 is the day of the [[goat]]; Zhēngyuè 5 is the day of the [[ox]]; Zhēngyuè 6 is the day of the [[horse]]; Zhēngyuè 7 is the day of the [[human]].
'''Lunisolar January''' (旧暦一月) is the first [[:en:Month|Month]] of the year in the {{ill|Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|ja|旧暦|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|11=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|qq=}} ([[:en:Lunisolar calendar|Lunisolar calendar]]).
Under definitions predating the [[:en:Tenpō calendar|Tenpō Calendar]], January was defined as the month that includes [[:en:Yushui (solar term)|Yushui]]. In the {{ill|New Calendar|ja|新暦|1=New Calendar (Japan)|lt=New Calendar|qq=draft|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/New Calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/New Calendar (Japan)|qq=}}, it corresponds approximately to late [[:en:January|January]] to early [[:en:March|March]].
Another name for January is '''Mutsuki''' (睦月). The etymology is explained at [[#Etymology of traditional Japanese name]]
In the Eastern lunisolar calendar, the number of days in a month, known as {{ill|Large and Small|ja|大小|qq=draft|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Large and Small|qq=}}, (with large months having 30 days and small months 29 days) varies from year to year. As a result, there are years when January ends on the 29th, with no January 30th.
==Etymology of traditional Japanese name== {{afc comment|1=content here taken from [[:ja:1月]][[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 00:08, 11 May 2025 (UTC)}} In [[:en:Japan|Japan]], the '''Lunar January''' is called '''Mutsuki''' (むつき), and today it is also used as an alternate name for January of the {{ill|Gregorian calendar|ja|新暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gregorian calendar|qq=}}. There are various theories about the origin of the name Mutsuki. The most widely accepted theory holds that it derives from “Mutsubizuki” (睦び月), meaning “month of harmony,” when all relatives gather to hold banquets. Other theories include “Mototsuki” (元つ月), “Moyutsuki” (萌月), and “Umutsuki” (生月).
In the Saru dialect of the {{ill|Hokkaido Ainu language|ja|北海道アイヌ語|lt=Hokkaido Ainu language|1=Ainu language|2=ja|3=アイヌ語|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hokkaido Ainu language|qq=}}, it is called “Towetanne” (トウエタンネ; [[:en:Ainu language|Ainu language]]: towetanne), meaning “the days become longer” (<ref>{{Cite web |title=アイヌ語カレンダー |url=https://www.ff-ainu.or.jp/calendardownload.html |website=(公益財団法人 アイヌ民族文化財団) |access-date=2025-01-12}}</ref>), and in the Asahikawa dialect, January is called “Inomi Cup” (イノミチュㇷ゚; [[:en:Ainu language|Ainu language]]: inomi cup), meaning “month of praying to the gods” (<ref>{{Cite web |title=月刊シロㇿ 10月号（2015.10） |url=https://ainugo.nam.go.jp/siror/monthly/201510.html#01 |website=アイヌと自然 デジタル図鑑 |access-date=2025-01-09 |publisher=[[:en:Ainu Museum|Ainu Museum]]}}</ref>). == Dates in Mutsuki == {| class="wikitable" |+ Dates in Mutsuki ! Year !! New Moon !! Last Day !! Number of Days |- | 2014 || [[:en:January 31|January 31]] || [[:en:February 28|February 28]] || 29 days |- | 2015 || [[:en:February 19|February 19]] || [[:en:March 19|March 19]] || 29 days |- | 2016 || [[:en:February 8|February 8]] || [[:en:March 8|March 8]] || 30 days |- | 2017 || [[:en:January 28|January 28]] || [[:en:February 25|February 25]] || 29 days |- | 2018 || [[:en:February 16|February 16]] || [[:en:March 16|March 16]] || 29 days |- | 2019 || [[:en:February 5|February 5]] || [[:en:March 6|March 6]] || 30 days |- | 2020 || [[:en:January 25|January 25]] || [[:en:February 23|February 23]] || 30 days |- | 2021 || [[:en:February 12|February 12]] || [[:en:March 12|March 12]] || 29 days |- | 2022 || [[:en:February 1|February 1]] || [[:en:March 2|March 2]] || 30 days |- | 2023 || [[:en:January 22|January 22]] || [[:en:February 19|February 19]] || 29 days |- | 2024 || [[:en:February 10|February 10]] || [[:en:March 9|March 9]] || 29 days |- | 2025 || [[:en:January 29|January 29]] || [[:en:February 27|February 27]] || 30 days |}
== Overview == {{unreferenced|date=January 2015|qq=}} {{Italic title|reason=|qq=}}
==Festivals== *[[Chinese New Year|New Year's Day]] ({{zh|c=春节|qq=}}) is Zhēngyuè 1. The following New Year's Day is January 29, 2025, which is a statutory [[holiday]]. *The [[Yuanxiao Festival]] ({{zh|c=元宵|qq=}}) is Zhēngyuè 15. The following Yuanxiao is February 12, 2025.
==Birth== *[[Wu Zetian]], the only [[female]] [[emperor]] of [[China]], Zhēngyuè 23, 624 *[[Li Bai]], a Chinese [[poet]] acclaimed to this day as a [[genius]] and romantic figure who took traditional [[poetry|poetic]] forms to new heights, Zhēngyuè 16, 701 *[[Emperor Shunzong of Tang]], an emperor of the Chinese [[Tang dynasty]], Zhēngyuè 12, 761 *[[Bai Juyi]], a Chinese [[poet]] of the Tang dynasty, Zhēngyuè 20, 772 *[[Emperor Xizong of Jin]], an emperor of the [[Jin dynasty (1115–1234)|Jin dynasty]] which controlled northern China from 1115 to 1234, Zhēngyuè 17, 1119 *[[Qiu Chuji]], a [[Daoist]] of [[Wang Chongyang]], Zhēngyuè 9, 1148 *[[Itō Suketaka]], a samurai, daimyō and twelfth family leader of the Itō clan, Zhēngyuè 15, 1559 *[[Li Hongzhang]], a [[politician]], [[general]], and [[diplomat]] of the late [[Qing Empire]], Zhēngyuè 5, 1823 *[[Aisin-Gioro Puyi]], the last emperor of China and the twelfth and final ruler of the [[Qing dynasty]], Zhēngyuè 14, 1906
==Death== *[[Sun Jian]], a military general and [[warlord]] who lived in the late [[Eastern Han dynasty]], Zhēngyuè 7, 191 *[[Guan Yu]], a general serving under the warlord [[Liu Bei]] in the late Eastern Han dynasty, Zhēngyuè 13, 220 *[[Cao Cao]], a warlord and the penultimate [[chancellor]] of the Eastern Han dynasty who rose to great power in the final years of the dynasty, Zhēngyuè 23, 220 *[[Emperor Xianzong of Tang]], an emperor of the Chinese Tang dynasty, Zhēngyuè 27, 820 *[[Emperor Yingzong of Song]], the fifth emperor of the [[Song dynasty]] of China, Zhēngyuè 7, 1067 *[[Emperor Zhezong of Song]], the seventh emperor of the Song dynasty of China, Zhēngyuè 12, 1100 *[[Kublai Khan]], the fifth [[Khagan]] (Great Khan) of the Ikh Mongol Uls ([[Mongol Empire]]), [[reign]]ing from 1260 to 1294, and the founder of the [[Yuan dynasty]] in [[Mongolia]] and China, Zhēngyuè 22, 1294 *[[Shunzhi Emperor]], the third emperor of the Qing dynasty and the first Qing emperor to rule over China, from 1644 to 1661, Zhēngyuè 30, 1638 *[[Qianlong Emperor]], the sixth emperor of the [[Manchu]]-led Qing dynasty, and the fourth Qing emperor to rule over China proper, Zhēngyuè 3, 1799 *[[Daoguang Emperor]], a Qing emperor, Zhēngyuè 14, 1850

revision.text:
{{Lunisolar January|qq=}} ''''Zhēngyuè''''' is the first [[month]] of the [[year]] in the [[Chinese calendar]]. In general, the Vernal Showers are in Zhēngyuè. The name comes from the annual [[morale]]-building [[Li (Confucian)|rite]] ({{zh|c=正旦大朝|qq=}}) in [[ancient China]].
Zhēngyuè 1 is the [[day]] of the [[rooster]]; Zhēngyuè 2 is the day of the [[dog]]; Zhēngyuè 3 is the day of the [[pig]]; Zhēngyuè 4 is the day of the [[goat]]; Zhēngyuè 5 is the day of the [[ox]]; Zhēngyuè 6 is the day of the [[horse]]; Zhēngyuè 7 is the day of the [[human]].
'''Lunisolar January''' (旧暦一月) is the first [[:en:Month|Month]] of the year in the {{ill|Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|ja|旧暦|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|11=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|qq=}} ([[:en:Lunisolar calendar|Lunisolar calendar]]).
Under definitions predating the [[:en:Tenpō calendar|Tenpō Calendar]], January was defined as the month that includes [[:en:Yushui (solar term)|Yushui]]. In the {{ill|New Calendar|ja|新暦|1=New Calendar (Japan)|lt=New Calendar|qq=draft|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/New Calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/New Calendar (Japan)|qq=}}, it corresponds approximately to late [[:en:January|January]] to early [[:en:March|March]].
Another name for January is '''Mutsuki''' (睦月). The etymology is explained at [[#Etymology of traditional Japanese name]]
In the Eastern lunisolar calendar, the number of days in a month, known as {{ill|Large and Small|ja|大小|qq=draft|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Large and Small|qq=}}, (with large months having 30 days and small months 29 days) varies from year to year. As a result, there are years when January ends on the 29th, with no January 30th.
==Etymology of traditional Japanese name== {{afc comment|1=content here taken from [[:ja:1月]][[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 00:08, 11 May 2025 (UTC)}} In [[:en:Japan|Japan]], the '''Lunar January''' is called '''Mutsuki''' (むつき), and today it is also used as an alternate name for January of the {{ill|Gregorian calendar|ja|新暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gregorian calendar|qq=}}. There are various theories about the origin of the name Mutsuki. The most widely accepted theory holds that it derives from “Mutsubizuki” (睦び月), meaning “month of harmony,” when all relatives gather to hold banquets. Other theories include “Mototsuki” (元つ月), “Moyutsuki” (萌月), and “Umutsuki” (生月).
In the Saru dialect of the {{ill|Hokkaido Ainu language|ja|北海道アイヌ語|lt=Hokkaido Ainu language|1=Ainu language|2=ja|3=アイヌ語|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hokkaido Ainu language|qq=}}, it is called “Towetanne” (トウエタンネ; [[:en:Ainu language|Ainu language]]: towetanne), meaning “the days become longer” (<ref>{{Cite web |title=アイヌ語カレンダー |url=https://www.ff-ainu.or.jp/calendardownload.html |website=(公益財団法人 アイヌ民族文化財団) |access-date=2025-01-12}}</ref>), and in the Asahikawa dialect, January is called “Inomi Cup” (イノミチュㇷ゚; [[:en:Ainu language|Ainu language]]: inomi cup), meaning “month of praying to the gods” (<ref>{{Cite web |title=月刊シロㇿ 10月号（2015.10） |url=https://ainugo.nam.go.jp/siror/monthly/201510.html#01 |website=アイヌと自然 デジタル図鑑 |access-date=2025-01-09 |publisher=[[:en:Ainu Museum|Ainu Museum]]}}</ref>). == Dates in Mutsuki == {| class="wikitable" |+ Dates in Mutsuki ! Year !! New Moon !! Last Day !! Number of Days |- | 2014 || [[:en:January 31|January 31]] || [[:en:February 28|February 28]] || 29 days |- | 2015 || [[:en:February 19|February 19]] || [[:en:March 19|March 19]] || 29 days |- | 2016 || [[:en:February 8|February 8]] || [[:en:March 8|March 8]] || 30 days |- | 2017 || [[:en:January 28|January 28]] || [[:en:February 25|February 25]] || 29 days |- | 2018 || [[:en:February 16|February 16]] || [[:en:March 16|March 16]] || 29 days |- | 2019 || [[:en:February 5|February 5]] || [[:en:March 6|March 6]] || 30 days |- | 2020 || [[:en:January 25|January 25]] || [[:en:February 23|February 23]] || 30 days |- | 2021 || [[:en:February 12|February 12]] || [[:en:March 12|March 12]] || 29 days |- | 2022 || [[:en:February 1|February 1]] || [[:en:March 2|March 2]] || 30 days |- | 2023 || [[:en:January 22|January 22]] || [[:en:February 19|February 19]] || 29 days |- | 2024 || [[:en:February 10|February 10]] || [[:en:March 9|March 9]] || 29 days |- | 2025 || [[:en:January 29|January 29]] || [[:en:February 27|February 27]] || 30 days |}
== Overview == {{unreferenced|date=January 2015|qq=}} {{Italic title|reason=|qq=}}
==Festivals== *[[Chinese New Year|New Year's Day]] ({{zh|c=春节|qq=}}) is Zhēngyuè 1. The following New Year's Day is January 29, 2025, which is a statutory [[holiday]]. *The [[Yuanxiao Festival]] ({{zh|c=元宵|qq=}}) is Zhēngyuè 15. The following Yuanxiao is February 12, 2025.
==Birth== *[[Wu Zetian]], the only [[female]] [[emperor]] of [[China]], Zhēngyuè 23, 624 *[[Li Bai]], a Chinese [[poet]] acclaimed to this day as a [[genius]] and romantic figure who took traditional [[poetry|poetic]] forms to new heights, Zhēngyuè 16, 701 *[[Emperor Shunzong of Tang]], an emperor of the Chinese [[Tang dynasty]], Zhēngyuè 12, 761 *[[Bai Juyi]], a Chinese [[poet]] of the Tang dynasty, Zhēngyuè 20, 772 *[[Emperor Xizong of Jin]], an emperor of the [[Jin dynasty (1115–1234)|Jin dynasty]] which controlled northern China from 1115 to 1234, Zhēngyuè 17, 1119 *[[Qiu Chuji]], a [[Daoist]] of [[Wang Chongyang]], Zhēngyuè 9, 1148 *[[Itō Suketaka]], a samurai, daimyō and twelfth family leader of the Itō clan, Zhēngyuè 15, 1559 *[[Li Hongzhang]], a [[politician]], [[general]], and [[diplomat]] of the late [[Qing Empire]], Zhēngyuè 5, 1823 *[[Aisin-Gioro Puyi]], the last emperor of China and the twelfth and final ruler of the [[Qing dynasty]], Zhēngyuè 14, 1906
==Death== *[[Sun Jian]], a military general and [[warlord]] who lived in the late [[Eastern Han dynasty]], Zhēngyuè 7, 191 *[[Guan Yu]], a general serving under the warlord [[Liu Bei]] in the late Eastern Han dynasty, Zhēngyuè 13, 220 *[[Cao Cao]], a warlord and the penultimate [[chancellor]] of the Eastern Han dynasty who rose to great power in the final years of the dynasty, Zhēngyuè 23, 220 *[[Emperor Xianzong of Tang]], an emperor of the Chinese Tang dynasty, Zhēngyuè 27, 820 *[[Emperor Yingzong of Song]], the fifth emperor of the [[Song dynasty]] of China, Zhēngyuè 7, 1067 *[[Emperor Zhezong of Song]], the seventh emperor of the Song dynasty of China, Zhēngyuè 12, 1100 *[[Kublai Khan]], the fifth [[Khagan]] (Great Khan) of the Ikh Mongol Uls ([[Mongol Empire]]), [[reign]]ing from 1260 to 1294, and the founder of the [[Yuan dynasty]] in [[Mongolia]] and China, Zhēngyuè 22, 1294 *[[Shunzhi Emperor]], the third emperor of the Qing dynasty and the first Qing emperor to rule over China, from 1644 to 1661, Zhēngyuè 30, 1638 *[[Qianlong Emperor]], the sixth emperor of the [[Manchu]]-led Qing dynasty, and the fourth Qing emperor to rule over China proper, Zhēngyuè 3, 1799 *[[Daoguang Emperor]], a Qing emperor, Zhēngyuè 14, 1850

== Overview == {{unreferenced|date=January 2015|qq=}} {{Italic title|reason=|qq=}}
==Festivals== *[[Chinese New Year|New Year's Day]] ({{zh|c=春节|qq=}}) is Zhēngyuè 1. The following New Year's Day is January 29, 2025, which is a statutory [[holiday]]. *The [[Yuanxiao Festival]] ({{zh|c=元宵|qq=}}) is Zhēngyuè 15. The following Yuanxiao is February 12, 2025.
==Birth== *[[Wu Zetian]], the only [[female]] [[emperor]] of [[China]], Zhēngyuè 23, 624 *[[Li Bai]], a Chinese [[poet]] acclaimed to this day as a [[genius]] and romantic figure who took traditional [[poetry|poetic]] forms to new heights, Zhēngyuè 16, 701 *[[Emperor Shunzong of Tang]], an emperor of the Chinese [[Tang dynasty]], Zhēngyuè 12, 761 *[[Bai Juyi]], a Chinese [[poet]] of the Tang dynasty, Zhēngyuè 20, 772 *[[Emperor Xizong of Jin]], an emperor of the [[Jin dynasty (1115–1234)|Jin dynasty]] which controlled northern China from 1115 to 1234, Zhēngyuè 17, 1119 *[[Qiu Chuji]], a [[Daoist]] of [[Wang Chongyang]], Zhēngyuè 9, 1148 *[[Itō Suketaka]], a samurai, daimyō and twelfth family leader of the Itō clan, Zhēngyuè 15, 1559 *[[Li Hongzhang]], a [[politician]], [[general]], and [[diplomat]] of the late [[Qing Empire]], Zhēngyuè 5, 1823 *[[Aisin-Gioro Puyi]], the last emperor of China and the twelfth and final ruler of the [[Qing dynasty]], Zhēngyuè 14, 1906
==Death== *[[Sun Jian]], a military general and [[warlord]] who lived in the late [[Eastern Han dynasty]], Zhēngyuè 7, 191 *[[Guan Yu]], a general serving under the warlord [[Liu Bei]] in the late Eastern Han dynasty, Zhēngyuè 13, 220 *[[Cao Cao]], a warlord and the penultimate [[chancellor]] of the Eastern Han dynasty who rose to great power in the final years of the dynasty, Zhēngyuè 23, 220 *[[Emperor Xianzong of Tang]], an emperor of the Chinese Tang dynasty, Zhēngyuè 27, 820 *[[Emperor Yingzong of Song]], the fifth emperor of the [[Song dynasty]] of China, Zhēngyuè 7, 1067 *[[Emperor Zhezong of Song]], the seventh emperor of the Song dynasty of China, Zhēngyuè 12, 1100 *[[Kublai Khan]], the fifth [[Khagan]] (Great Khan) of the Ikh Mongol Uls ([[Mongol Empire]]), [[reign]]ing from 1260 to 1294, and the founder of the [[Yuan dynasty]] in [[Mongolia]] and China, Zhēngyuè 22, 1294 *[[Shunzhi Emperor]], the third emperor of the Qing dynasty and the first Qing emperor to rule over China, from 1644 to 1661, Zhēngyuè 30, 1638 *[[Qianlong Emperor]], the sixth emperor of the [[Manchu]]-led Qing dynasty, and the fourth Qing emperor to rule over China proper, Zhēngyuè 3, 1799 *[[Daoguang Emperor]], a Qing emperor, Zhēngyuè 14, 1850

{{Lunisolar January|qq=}} ''''Zhēngyuè''''' is the first [[month]] of the [[year]] in the [[Chinese calendar]]. In general, the Vernal Showers are in Zhēngyuè. The name comes from the annual [[morale]]-building [[Li (Confucian)|rite]] ({{zh|c=正旦大朝|qq=}}) in [[ancient China]].
Zhēngyuè 1 is the [[day]] of the [[rooster]]; Zhēngyuè 2 is the day of the [[dog]]; Zhēngyuè 3 is the day of the [[pig]]; Zhēngyuè 4 is the day of the [[goat]]; Zhēngyuè 5 is the day of the [[ox]]; Zhēngyuè 6 is the day of the [[horse]]; Zhēngyuè 7 is the day of the [[human]].
'''Lunisolar January''' (旧暦一月) is the first [[:en:Month|Month]] of the year in the {{ill|Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|ja|旧暦|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|11=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|qq=}} ([[:en:Lunisolar calendar|Lunisolar calendar]]).
Under definitions predating the [[:en:Tenpō calendar|Tenpō Calendar]], January was defined as the month that includes [[:en:Yushui (solar term)|Yushui]]. In the {{ill|New Calendar|ja|新暦|1=New Calendar (Japan)|lt=New Calendar|qq=draft|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/New Calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/New Calendar (Japan)|qq=}}, it corresponds approximately to late [[:en:January|January]] to early [[:en:March|March]].
Another name for January is '''Mutsuki''' (睦月). The etymology is explained at [[#Etymology of traditional Japanese name]]
In the Eastern lunisolar calendar, the number of days in a month, known as {{ill|Large and Small|ja|大小|qq=draft|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Large and Small|qq=}}, (with large months having 30 days and small months 29 days) varies from year to year. As a result, there are years when January ends on the 29th, with no January 30th.
==Etymology of traditional Japanese name== {{afc comment|1=content here taken from [[:ja:1月]][[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 00:08, 11 May 2025 (UTC)}} In [[:en:Japan|Japan]], the '''Lunar January''' is called '''Mutsuki''' (むつき), and today it is also used as an alternate name for January of the {{ill|Gregorian calendar|ja|新暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gregorian calendar|qq=}}. There are various theories about the origin of the name Mutsuki. The most widely accepted theory holds that it derives from “Mutsubizuki” (睦び月), meaning “month of harmony,” when all relatives gather to hold banquets. Other theories include “Mototsuki” (元つ月), “Moyutsuki” (萌月), and “Umutsuki” (生月).
In the Saru dialect of the [[:en:Ainu language|Hokkaido Ainu language]], it is called “Towetanne” (トウエタンネ; [[:en:Ainu language|Ainu language]]: towetanne), meaning “the days become longer” (<ref>{{Cite web |title=アイヌ語カレンダー |url=https://www.ff-ainu.or.jp/calendardownload.html |website=(公益財団法人 アイヌ民族文化財団) |access-date=2025-01-12}}</ref>), and in the Asahikawa dialect, January is called “Inomi Cup” (イノミチュㇷ゚; [[:en:Ainu language|Ainu language]]: inomi cup), meaning “month of praying to the gods” (<ref>{{Cite web |title=月刊シロㇿ 10月号（2015.10） |url=https://ainugo.nam.go.jp/siror/monthly/201510.html#01 |website=アイヌと自然 デジタル図鑑 |access-date=2025-01-09 |publisher=[[:en:Ainu Museum|Ainu Museum]]}}</ref>). == Dates in Mutsuki == {| class="wikitable" |+ Dates in Mutsuki ! Year !! New Moon !! Last Day !! Number of Days |- | 2014 || [[:en:January 31|January 31]] || [[:en:February 28|February 28]] || 29 days |- | 2015 || [[:en:February 19|February 19]] || [[:en:March 19|March 19]] || 29 days |- | 2016 || [[:en:February 8|February 8]] || [[:en:March 8|March 8]] || 30 days |- | 2017 || [[:en:January 28|January 28]] || [[:en:February 25|February 25]] || 29 days |- | 2018 || [[:en:February 16|February 16]] || [[:en:March 16|March 16]] || 29 days |- | 2019 || [[:en:February 5|February 5]] || [[:en:March 6|March 6]] || 30 days |- | 2020 || [[:en:January 25|January 25]] || [[:en:February 23|February 23]] || 30 days |- | 2021 || [[:en:February 12|February 12]] || [[:en:March 12|March 12]] || 29 days |- | 2022 || [[:en:February 1|February 1]] || [[:en:March 2|March 2]] || 30 days |- | 2023 || [[:en:January 22|January 22]] || [[:en:February 19|February 19]] || 29 days |- | 2024 || [[:en:February 10|February 10]] || [[:en:March 9|March 9]] || 29 days |- | 2025 || [[:en:January 29|January 29]] || [[:en:February 27|February 27]] || 30 days |}
== Overview == {{unreferenced|date=January 2015|qq=}} {{Italic title|reason=|qq=}}
==Festivals== *[[Chinese New Year|New Year's Day]] ({{zh|c=春节|qq=}}) is Zhēngyuè 1. The following New Year's Day is January 29, 2025, which is a statutory [[holiday]]. *The [[Yuanxiao Festival]] ({{zh|c=元宵|qq=}}) is Zhēngyuè 15. The following Yuanxiao is February 12, 2025.
==Birth== *[[Wu Zetian]], the only [[female]] [[emperor]] of [[China]], Zhēngyuè 23, 624 *[[Li Bai]], a Chinese [[poet]] acclaimed to this day as a [[genius]] and romantic figure who took traditional [[poetry|poetic]] forms to new heights, Zhēngyuè 16, 701 *[[Emperor Shunzong of Tang]], an emperor of the Chinese [[Tang dynasty]], Zhēngyuè 12, 761 *[[Bai Juyi]], a Chinese [[poet]] of the Tang dynasty, Zhēngyuè 20, 772 *[[Emperor Xizong of Jin]], an emperor of the [[Jin dynasty (1115–1234)|Jin dynasty]] which controlled northern China from 1115 to 1234, Zhēngyuè 17, 1119 *[[Qiu Chuji]], a [[Daoist]] of [[Wang Chongyang]], Zhēngyuè 9, 1148 *[[Itō Suketaka]], a samurai, daimyō and twelfth family leader of the Itō clan, Zhēngyuè 15, 1559 *[[Li Hongzhang]], a [[politician]], [[general]], and [[diplomat]] of the late [[Qing Empire]], Zhēngyuè 5, 1823 *[[Aisin-Gioro Puyi]], the last emperor of China and the twelfth and final ruler of the [[Qing dynasty]], Zhēngyuè 14, 1906
==Death== *[[Sun Jian]], a military general and [[warlord]] who lived in the late [[Eastern Han dynasty]], Zhēngyuè 7, 191 *[[Guan Yu]], a general serving under the warlord [[Liu Bei]] in the late Eastern Han dynasty, Zhēngyuè 13, 220 *[[Cao Cao]], a warlord and the penultimate [[chancellor]] of the Eastern Han dynasty who rose to great power in the final years of the dynasty, Zhēngyuè 23, 220 *[[Emperor Xianzong of Tang]], an emperor of the Chinese Tang dynasty, Zhēngyuè 27, 820 *[[Emperor Yingzong of Song]], the fifth emperor of the [[Song dynasty]] of China, Zhēngyuè 7, 1067 *[[Emperor Zhezong of Song]], the seventh emperor of the Song dynasty of China, Zhēngyuè 12, 1100 *[[Kublai Khan]], the fifth [[Khagan]] (Great Khan) of the Ikh Mongol Uls ([[Mongol Empire]]), [[reign]]ing from 1260 to 1294, and the founder of the [[Yuan dynasty]] in [[Mongolia]] and China, Zhēngyuè 22, 1294 *[[Shunzhi Emperor]], the third emperor of the Qing dynasty and the first Qing emperor to rule over China, from 1644 to 1661, Zhēngyuè 30, 1638 *[[Qianlong Emperor]], the sixth emperor of the [[Manchu]]-led Qing dynasty, and the fourth Qing emperor to rule over China proper, Zhēngyuè 3, 1799 *[[Daoguang Emperor]], a Qing emperor, Zhēngyuè 14, 1850

'''Lunisolar January''' (旧暦一月) is the first [[:en:Month|Month]] of the year in the {{ill|Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|ja|旧暦|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|11=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|qq=}} ([[:en:Lunisolar calendar|Lunisolar calendar]]).
Under definitions predating the [[:en:Tenpō calendar|Tenpō Calendar]], January was defined as the month that includes [[:en:Yushui (solar term)|Yushui]]. In the {{ill|New Calendar|ja|新暦|1=New Calendar (Japan)|lt=New Calendar|qq=draft|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/New Calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/New Calendar (Japan)|qq=}}, it corresponds approximately to late [[:en:January|January]] to early [[:en:March|March]].
Another name for January is '''Mutsuki''' (睦月). The etymology is explained at [[#Etymology of traditional Japanese name]]

As a result, there are years when January ends on the 29th, with no January 30th.
==Etymology of traditional Japanese name== {{afc comment|1=content here taken from [[:ja:1月]][[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 00:08, 11 May 2025 (UTC)}} In [[:en:Japan|Japan]], the '''Lunar January''' is called '''Mutsuki''' (むつき), and today it is also used as an alternate name for January of the {{ill|Gregorian calendar|ja|新暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gregorian calendar|qq=}}. Other theories include “Mototsuki” (元つ月), “Moyutsuki” (萌月), and “Umutsuki” (生月).
In the Saru dialect of the [[:en:Ainu language|Hokkaido Ainu language]], it is called “Towetanne” (トウエタンネ; [[:en:Ainu language|Ainu language]]: towetanne), meaning “the days become longer” (<ref>{{Cite web |title=アイヌ語カレンダー |url=https://www.ff-ainu.or.jp/calendardownload.html |website=(公益財団法人 アイヌ民族文化財団) |access-date=2025-01-12}}</ref>), and in the Asahikawa dialect, January is called “Inomi Cup” (イノミチュㇷ゚; [[:en:Ainu language|Ainu language]]: inomi cup), meaning “month of praying to the gods” (<ref>{{Cite web |title=月刊シロㇿ 10月号（2015.10） |url=https://ainugo.nam.go.jp/siror/monthly/201510.html#01 |website=アイヌと自然 デジタル図鑑 |access-date=2025-01-09 |publisher=[[:en:Ainu Museum|Ainu Museum]]}}</ref>). == Dates in Mutsuki == {| class="wikitable" |+ Dates in Mutsuki ! {{Lunisolar January|qq=}} ''''Zhēngyuè''''' is the first [[month]] of the [[year]] in the [[Chinese calendar]]. In general, the Vernal Showers are in Zhēngyuè. The name comes from the annual [[morale]]-building [[Li (Confucian)|rite]] ({{zh|c=正旦大朝|qq=}}) in [[ancient China]].
Zhēngyuè 1 is the [[day]] of the [[rooster]]; Zhēngyuè 2 is the day of the [[dog]]; Zhēngyuè 3 is the day of the [[pig]]; Zhēngyuè 4 is the day of the [[goat]]; Zhēngyuè 5 is the day of the [[ox]]; Zhēngyuè 6 is the day of the [[horse]]; Zhēngyuè 7 is the day of the [[human]].
'''Lunisolar January''' (旧暦一月) is the first [[:en:Month|Month]] of the year in the {{ill|Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|ja|旧暦|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|11=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|qq=}} ([[:en:Lunisolar calendar|Lunisolar calendar]]).
Under definitions predating the [[:en:Tenpō calendar|Tenpō Calendar]], January was defined as the month that includes [[:en:Yushui (solar term)|Yushui]]. In the {{ill|New Calendar|ja|新暦|1=New Calendar (Japan)|lt=New Calendar|qq=draft|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/New Calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/New Calendar (Japan)|qq=}}, it corresponds approximately to late [[:en:January|January]] to early [[:en:March|March]].
Another name for January is '''Mutsuki''' (睦月). The etymology is explained at [[#Etymology of traditional Japanese name]]
In the Eastern lunisolar calendar, the number of days in a month, known as {{ill|Large and Small|ja|大小|qq=draft|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Large and Small|qq=}}, (with large months having 30 days and small months 29 days) varies from year to year. As a result, there are years when January ends on the 29th, with no January 30th.
==Etymology of traditional Japanese name== {{afc comment|1=content here taken from [[:ja:1月]][[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 00:08, 11 May 2025 (UTC)}} In [[:en:Japan|Japan]], the '''Lunar January''' is called '''Mutsuki''' (むつき), and today it is also used as an alternate name for January of the {{ill|Gregorian calendar|ja|新暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gregorian calendar|qq=}}. There are various theories about the origin of the name Mutsuki. The most widely accepted theory holds that it derives from “Mutsubizuki” (睦び月), meaning “month of harmony,” when all relatives gather to hold banquets. Other theories include “Mototsuki” (元つ月), “Moyutsuki” (萌月), and “Umutsuki” (生月).
In the Saru dialect of the [[:en:Ainu language|Hokkaido Ainu language]], it is called “Towetanne” (トウエタンネ; [[:en:Ainu language|Ainu language]]: towetanne), meaning “the days become longer” (<ref>{{Cite web |title=アイヌ語カレンダー |url=https://www.ff-ainu.or.jp/calendardownload.html |website=(公益財団法人 アイヌ民族文化財団) |access-date=2025-01-12}}</ref>), and in the Asahikawa dialect, January is called “Inomi Cup” (イノミチュㇷ゚; [[:en:Ainu language|Ainu language]]: inomi cup), meaning “month of praying to the gods” (<ref>{{Cite web |title=月刊シロㇿ 10月号（2015.10） |url=https://ainugo.nam.go.jp/siror/monthly/201510.html#01 |website=アイヌと自然 デジタル図鑑 |access-date=2025-01-09 |publisher=[[:en:Ainu Museum|Ainu Museum]]}}</ref>). == Dates in Mutsuki == {| class="wikitable" |+ Dates in Mutsuki ! Year !! New Moon !! Last Day !! Number of Days |- | 2014 || [[:en:January 31|January 31]] || [[:en:February 28|February 28]] || 29 days |- | 2015 || [[:en:February 19|February 19]] || [[:en:March 19|March 19]] || 29 days |- | 2016 || [[:en:February 8|February 8]] || [[:en:March 8|March 8]] || 30 days |- | 2017 || [[:en:January 28|January 28]] || [[:en:February 25|February 25]] || 29 days |- | 2018 || [[:en:February 16|February 16]] || [[:en:March 16|March 16]] || 29 days |- | 2019 || [[:en:February 5|February 5]] || [[:en:March 6|March 6]] || 30 days |- | 2020 || [[:en:January 25|January 25]] || [[:en:February 23|February 23]] || 30 days |- | 2021 || [[:en:February 12|February 12]] || [[:en:March 12|March 12]] || 29 days |- | 2022 || [[:en:February 1|February 1]] || [[:en:March 2|March 2]] || 30 days |- | 2023 || [[:en:January 22|January 22]] || [[:en:February 19|February 19]] || 29 days |- | 2024 || [[:en:February 10|February 10]] || [[:en:March 9|March 9]] || 29 days |- | 2025 || [[:en:January 29|January 29]] || [[:en:February 27|February 27]] || 30 days |}
== Overview == {{unreferenced|date=January 2015|qq=}} {{Italic title|reason=|qq=}}
==Festivals== *[[Chinese New Year|New Year's Day]] ({{zh|c=春节|qq=}}) is Zhēngyuè 1. The following New Year's Day is January 29, 2025, which is a statutory [[holiday]]. *The [[Yuanxiao Festival]] ({{zh|c=元宵|qq=}}) is Zhēngyuè 15. The following Yuanxiao is February 12, 2025.
==Birth== *[[Wu Zetian]], the only [[female]] [[emperor]] of [[China]], Zhēngyuè 23, 624 *[[Li Bai]], a Chinese [[poet]] acclaimed to this day as a [[genius]] and romantic figure who took traditional [[poetry|poetic]] forms to new heights, Zhēngyuè 16, 701 *[[Emperor Shunzong of Tang]], an emperor of the Chinese [[Tang dynasty]], Zhēngyuè 12, 761 *[[Bai Juyi]], a Chinese [[poet]] of the Tang dynasty, Zhēngyuè 20, 772 *[[Emperor Xizong of Jin]], an emperor of the [[Jin dynasty (1115–1234)|Jin dynasty]] which controlled northern China from 1115 to 1234, Zhēngyuè 17, 1119 *[[Qiu Chuji]], a [[Daoist]] of [[Wang Chongyang]], Zhēngyuè 9, 1148 *[[Itō Suketaka]], a samurai, daimyō and twelfth family leader of the Itō clan, Zhēngyuè 15, 1559 *[[Li Hongzhang]], a [[politician]], [[general]], and [[diplomat]] of the late [[Qing Empire]], Zhēngyuè 5, 1823 *[[Aisin-Gioro Puyi]], the last emperor of China and the twelfth and final ruler of the [[Qing dynasty]], Zhēngyuè 14, 1906
==Death== *[[Sun Jian]], a military general and [[warlord]] who lived in the late [[Eastern Han dynasty]], Zhēngyuè 7, 191 *[[Guan Yu]], a general serving under the warlord [[Liu Bei]] in the late Eastern Han dynasty, Zhēngyuè 13, 220 *[[Cao Cao]], a warlord and the penultimate [[chancellor]] of the Eastern Han dynasty who rose to great power in the final years of the dynasty, Zhēngyuè 23, 220 *[[Emperor Xianzong of Tang]], an emperor of the Chinese Tang dynasty, Zhēngyuè 27, 820 *[[Emperor Yingzong of Song]], the fifth emperor of the [[Song dynasty]] of China, Zhēngyuè 7, 1067 *[[Emperor Zhezong of Song]], the seventh emperor of the Song dynasty of China, Zhēngyuè 12, 1100 *[[Kublai Khan]], the fifth [[Khagan]] (Great Khan) of the Ikh Mongol Uls ([[Mongol Empire]]), [[reign]]ing from 1260 to 1294, and the founder of the [[Yuan dynasty]] in [[Mongolia]] and China, Zhēngyuè 22, 1294 *[[Shunzhi Emperor]], the third emperor of the Qing dynasty and the first Qing emperor to rule over China, from 1644 to 1661, Zhēngyuè 30, 1638 *[[Qianlong Emperor]], the sixth emperor of the [[Manchu]]-led Qing dynasty, and the fourth Qing emperor to rule over China proper, Zhēngyuè 3, 1799 *[[Daoguang Emperor]], a Qing emperor, Zhēngyuè 14, 1850

{{Lunisolar January|qq=}} ''''Zhēngyuè''''' is the first [[month]] of the [[year]] in the [[Chinese calendar]]. In general, the Vernal Showers are in Zhēngyuè. The name comes from the annual [[morale]]-building [[Li (Confucian)|rite]] ({{zh|c=正旦大朝|qq=}}) in [[ancient China]].
Zhēngyuè 1 is the [[day]] of the [[rooster]]; Zhēngyuè 2 is the day of the [[dog]]; Zhēngyuè 3 is the day of the [[pig]]; Zhēngyuè 4 is the day of the [[goat]]; Zhēngyuè 5 is the day of the [[ox]]; Zhēngyuè 6 is the day of the [[horse]]; Zhēngyuè 7 is the day of the [[human]].
'''Lunisolar January''' (旧暦一月) is the first [[:en:Month|Month]] of the year in the {{ill|Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|ja|旧暦|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|11=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|qq=}} ([[:en:Lunisolar calendar|Lunisolar calendar]]).
Under definitions predating the [[:en:Tenpō calendar|Tenpō Calendar]], January was defined as the month that includes [[:en:Yushui (solar term)|Yushui]]. In the {{ill|New Calendar|ja|新暦|1=New Calendar (Japan)|lt=New Calendar|qq=draft|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/New Calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/New Calendar (Japan)|qq=}}, it corresponds approximately to late [[:en:January|January]] to early [[:en:March|March]].
Another name for January is '''Mutsuki''' (睦月). The etymology is explained at [[#Etymology of traditional Japanese name]]
In the Eastern lunisolar calendar, the number of days in a month, known as {{ill|Large and Small|ja|大小|qq=draft|qq=|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Large and Small|qq=}}, (with large months having 30 days and small months 29 days) varies from year to year. As a result, there are years when January ends on the 29th, with no January 30th.
==Etymology of traditional Japanese name== {{afc comment|1=content here taken from [[:ja:1月]][[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 00:08, 11 May 2025 (UTC)}} In [[:en:Japan|Japan]], the '''Lunar January''' is called '''Mutsuki''' (むつき), and today it is also used as an alternate name for January of the {{ill|Gregorian calendar|ja|新暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gregorian calendar|qq=}}. There are various theories about the origin of the name Mutsuki. The most widely accepted theory holds that it derives from “Mutsubizuki” (睦び月), meaning “month of harmony,” when all relatives gather to hold banquets. Other theories include “Mototsuki” (元つ月), “Moyutsuki” (萌月), and “Umutsuki” (生月).
In the Saru dialect of the [[:en:Ainu language|Hokkaido Ainu language]], it is called “Towetanne” (トウエタンネ; [[:en:Ainu language|Ainu language]]: towetanne), meaning “the days become longer” (<ref>{{Cite web |title=アイヌ語カレンダー |url=https://www.ff-ainu.or.jp/calendardownload.html |website=(公益財団法人 アイヌ民族文化財団) |access-date=2025-01-12}}</ref>), and in the Asahikawa dialect, January is called “Inomi Cup” (イノミチュㇷ゚; [[:en:Ainu language|Ainu language]]: inomi cup), meaning “month of praying to the gods” (<ref>{{Cite web |title=月刊シロㇿ 10月号（2015.10） |url=https://ainugo.nam.go.jp/siror/monthly/201510.html#01 |website=アイヌと自然 デジタル図鑑 |access-date=2025-01-09 |publisher=[[:en:Ainu Museum|Ainu Museum]]}}</ref>). == Dates in Mutsuki == {| class="wikitable" |+ Dates in Mutsuki ! ==Festivals== *[[Chinese New Year|New Year's Day]] ({{zh|c=春节|qq=}}) is Zhēngyuè 1. The following New Year's Day is January 29, 2025, which is a statutory [[holiday]]. *The [[Yuanxiao Festival]] ({{zh|c=元宵|qq=}}) is Zhēngyuè 15. The following Yuanxiao is February 12, 2025.
==Birth== *[[Wu Zetian]], the only [[female]] [[emperor]] of [[China]], Zhēngyuè 23, 624 *[[Li Bai]], a Chinese [[poet]] acclaimed to this day as a [[genius]] and romantic figure who took traditional [[poetry|poetic]] forms to new heights, Zhēngyuè 16, 701 *[[Emperor Shunzong of Tang]], an emperor of the Chinese [[Tang dynasty]], Zhēngyuè 12, 761 *[[Bai Juyi]], a Chinese [[poet]] of the Tang dynasty, Zhēngyuè 20, 772 *[[Emperor Xizong of Jin]], an emperor of the [[Jin dynasty (1115–1234)|Jin dynasty]] which controlled northern China from 1115 to 1234, Zhēngyuè 17, 1119 *[[Qiu Chuji]], a [[Daoist]] of [[Wang Chongyang]], Zhēngyuè 9, 1148 *[[Itō Suketaka]], a samurai, daimyō and twelfth family leader of the Itō clan, Zhēngyuè 15, 1559 *[[Li Hongzhang]], a [[politician]], [[general]], and [[diplomat]] of the late [[Qing Empire]], Zhēngyuè 5, 1823 *[[:en:Aisin-Gioro Puyi|Aisin-Gioro Puyi]], the last emperor of China and the twelfth and final ruler of the [[Qing dynasty]], Zhēngyuè 14, 1906
==Death== *[[Sun Jian]], a military general and [[warlord]] who lived in the late [[Eastern Han dynasty]], Zhēngyuè 7, 191 *[[Guan Yu]], a general serving under the warlord [[Liu Bei]] in the late Eastern Han dynasty, Zhēngyuè 13, 220 *[[Cao Cao]], a warlord and the penultimate [[chancellor]] of the Eastern Han dynasty who rose to great power in the final years of the dynasty, Zhēngyuè 23, 220 *[[Emperor Xianzong of Tang]], an emperor of the Chinese Tang dynasty, Zhēngyuè 27, 820 *[[Emperor Yingzong of Song]], the fifth emperor of the [[Song dynasty]] of China, Zhēngyuè 7, 1067 *[[Emperor Zhezong of Song]], the seventh emperor of the Song dynasty of China, Zhēngyuè 12, 1100 *[[Kublai Khan]], the fifth [[Khagan]] (Great Khan) of the Ikh Mongol Uls ([[Mongol Empire]]), [[reign]]ing from 1260 to 1294, and the founder of the [[Yuan dynasty]] in [[Mongolia]] and China, Zhēngyuè 22, 1294 *[[Shunzhi Emperor]], the third emperor of the Qing dynasty and the first Qing emperor to rule over China, from 1644 to 1661, Zhēngyuè 30, 1638 *[[Qianlong Emperor]], the sixth emperor of the [[Manchu]]-led Qing dynasty, and the fourth Qing emperor to rule over China proper, Zhēngyuè 3, 1799 *[[Daoguang Emperor]], a Qing emperor, Zhēngyuè 14, 1850

== Overview == {{unreferenced|date=January 2015|qq=}} {{Italic title|reason=|qq=}}
==Festivals== *[[Chinese New Year|New Year's Day]] ({{zh|c=春节|qq=}}) is Zhēngyuè 1. The following New Year's Day is January 29, 2025, which is a statutory [[holiday]]. *The [[Yuanxiao Festival]] ({{zh|c=元宵|qq=}}) is Zhēngyuè 15. The following Yuanxiao is February 12, 2025.
==Birth== *[[Wu Zetian]], the only [[female]] [[emperor]] of [[China]], Zhēngyuè 23, 624 *[[Li Bai]], a Chinese [[poet]] acclaimed to this day as a [[genius]] and romantic figure who took traditional [[poetry|poetic]] forms to new heights, Zhēngyuè 16, 701 *[[Emperor Shunzong of Tang]], an emperor of the Chinese [[Tang dynasty]], Zhēngyuè 12, 761 *[[Bai Juyi]], a Chinese [[poet]] of the Tang dynasty, Zhēngyuè 20, 772 *[[Emperor Xizong of Jin]], an emperor of the [[Jin dynasty (1115–1234)|Jin dynasty]] which controlled northern China from 1115 to 1234, Zhēngyuè 17, 1119 *[[Qiu Chuji]], a [[Daoist]] of [[Wang Chongyang]], Zhēngyuè 9, 1148 *[[Itō Suketaka]], a samurai, daimyō and twelfth family leader of the Itō clan, Zhēngyuè 15, 1559 *[[Li Hongzhang]], a [[politician]], [[general]], and [[diplomat]] of the late [[Qing Empire]], Zhēngyuè 5, 1823 *[[:en:Aisin-Gioro Puyi|Aisin-Gioro Puyi]], the last emperor of China and the twelfth and final ruler of the [[Qing dynasty]], Zhēngyuè 14, 1906
==Death== *[[Sun Jian]], a military general and [[warlord]] who lived in the late [[Eastern Han dynasty]], Zhēngyuè 7, 191 *[[Guan Yu]], a general serving under the warlord [[Liu Bei]] in the late Eastern Han dynasty, Zhēngyuè 13, 220 *[[Cao Cao]], a warlord and the penultimate [[chancellor]] of the Eastern Han dynasty who rose to great power in the final years of the dynasty, Zhēngyuè 23, 220 *[[Emperor Xianzong of Tang]], an emperor of the Chinese Tang dynasty, Zhēngyuè 27, 820 *[[Emperor Yingzong of Song]], the fifth emperor of the [[Song dynasty]] of China, Zhēngyuè 7, 1067 *[[Emperor Zhezong of Song]], the seventh emperor of the Song dynasty of China, Zhēngyuè 12, 1100 *[[Kublai Khan]], the fifth [[Khagan]] (Great Khan) of the Ikh Mongol Uls ([[Mongol Empire]]), [[reign]]ing from 1260 to 1294, and the founder of the [[Yuan dynasty]] in [[Mongolia]] and China, Zhēngyuè 22, 1294 *[[Shunzhi Emperor]], the third emperor of the Qing dynasty and the first Qing emperor to rule over China, from 1644 to 1661, Zhēngyuè 30, 1638 *[[Qianlong Emperor]], the sixth emperor of the [[Manchu]]-led Qing dynasty, and the fourth Qing emperor to rule over China proper, Zhēngyuè 3, 1799 *[[Daoguang Emperor]], a Qing emperor, Zhēngyuè 14, 1850
