== Overview == {{unreferenced|date=January 2015|qq=}} {{Italic title|reason=|qq=}}
==Festivals== *{{ill|Chinese New Year|en|Chinese New Year|WD=Q131772|lt=New Year's Day}} ({{zh|c=春节|qq=}}) is Zhēngyuè 1. The following New Year's Day is January 29, 2025, which is a statutory [[holiday]]. *The [[:en:Yuanxiao Festival|Yuanxiao Festival]] ({{zh|c=元宵|qq=}}) is Zhēngyuè 15. The following Yuanxiao is February 12, 2025.
==Birth== *{{ill|Wu Zetian|en|Wu Zetian|WD=Q9738|lt=Wu Zetian}}, the only {{ill|Female|en|Female|WD=Q43445|lt=Female}} {{ill|Emperor|en|Emperor|WD=Q39018|lt=Emperor}} of {{ill|China|en|China|WD=Q148|lt=China}}, Zhēngyuè 23, 624 *{{ill|en:Li Bai|en|en:Li Bai|WD=Q7071|lt=en:Li Bai}}, a Chinese {{ill|Poet|en|Poet|WD=Q49757|lt=Poet}} acclaimed to this day as a {{ill|genius|en|genius|WD=Q185030|lt=genius}} and romantic figure who took traditional {{ill|Poetry|en|Poetry|WD=Q482|lt=poetic}} forms to new heights, Zhēngyuè 16, 701 *{{ill|Emperor Shunzong of Tang|en|Emperor Shunzong of Tang|WD=Q9760|lt=Emperor Shunzong of Tang}}, an emperor of the Chinese {{ill|Tang dynasty|en|Tang dynasty|WD=Q9683|lt=Tang dynasty}}, Zhēngyuè 12, 761 *{{ill|Bai Juyi|en|Bai Juyi|WD=Q313324|lt=Bai Juyi}}, a Chinese {{ill|Poet|en|Poet|WD=Q49757|lt=Poet}} of the Tang dynasty, Zhēngyuè 20, 772 *{{ill|Emperor Xizong of Jin|en|Emperor Xizong of Jin|WD=Q5071|lt=Emperor Xizong of Jin}}, an emperor of the {{ill|Jin dynasty (1115–1234)|en|Jin dynasty (1115–1234)|WD=Q5066|lt=Jin dynasty}} which controlled northern China from 1115 to 1234, Zhēngyuè 17, 1119 *{{ill|Qiu Chuji|en|Qiu Chuji|WD=Q565148|lt=Qiu Chuji}}, a [[:en:Daoist|Daoist]] of {{ill|Wang Chongyang|en|Wang Chongyang|WD=Q701186|lt=Wang Chongyang}}, Zhēngyuè 9, 1148 *{{ill|Itō Suketaka|en|Itō Suketaka|WD=Q8014919|lt=Itō Suketaka}}, a samurai, daimyō and twelfth family leader of the Itō clan, Zhēngyuè 15, 1559 *{{ill|Li Hongzhang|en|Li Hongzhang|WD=Q378517|lt=Li Hongzhang}}, a {{ill|Politician|en|Politician|WD=Q82955|lt=Politician}}, {{ill|General|en|General|WD=Q83460|lt=General}}, and {{ill|Diplomat|en|Diplomat|WD=Q193391|lt=Diplomat}} of the late [[:en:Qing Empire|Qing Empire]], Zhēngyuè 5, 1823 *[[:en:Aisin-Gioro Puyi|Aisin-Gioro Puyi]], the last emperor of China and the twelfth and final ruler of the {{ill|Qing dynasty|en|Qing dynasty|WD=Q8733|lt=Qing dynasty}}, Zhēngyuè 14, 1906
==Death== *{{ill|Sun Jian|en|Sun Jian|WD=Q334101|lt=Sun Jian}}, a military general and {{ill|Warlord|en|Warlord|WD=Q220098|lt=Warlord}} who lived in the late [[:en:Eastern Han dynasty|Eastern Han dynasty]], Zhēngyuè 7, 191 *{{ill|Guan Yu|en|Guan Yu|WD=Q222797|lt=Guan Yu}}, a general serving under the warlord {{ill|Liu Bei|en|Liu Bei|WD=Q245315|lt=Liu Bei}} in the late Eastern Han dynasty, Zhēngyuè 13, 220 *{{ill|Cao Cao|en|Cao Cao|WD=Q204077|lt=Cao Cao}}, a warlord and the penultimate {{ill|Chancellor|en|Chancellor|WD=Q373085|lt=Chancellor}} of the Eastern Han dynasty who rose to great power in the final years of the dynasty, Zhēngyuè 23, 220 *{{ill|Emperor Xianzong of Tang|en|Emperor Xianzong of Tang|WD=Q9761|lt=Emperor Xianzong of Tang}}, an emperor of the Chinese Tang dynasty, Zhēngyuè 27, 820 *{{ill|Emperor Yingzong of Song|en|Emperor Yingzong of Song|WD=Q7479|lt=Emperor Yingzong of Song}}, the fifth emperor of the {{ill|Song dynasty|en|Song dynasty|WD=Q7462|lt=Song dynasty}} of China, Zhēngyuè 7, 1067 *[[:en:Emperor Zhezong of Song|Emperor Zhezong of Song]], the seventh emperor of the Song dynasty of China, Zhēngyuè 12, 1100 *{{ill|Kublai Khan|en|Kublai Khan|WD=Q7523|lt=Kublai Khan}}, the fifth {{ill|Khagan|en|Khagan|WD=Q187878|lt=Khagan}} (Great Khan) of the Ikh Mongol Uls ({{ill|Mongol Empire|en|Mongol Empire|WD=Q12557|lt=Mongol Empire}}), {{ill|reign|en|reign|WD=Q7310094|lt=reign}}ing from 1260 to 1294, and the founder of the {{ill|Yuan dynasty|en|Yuan dynasty|WD=Q7313|lt=Yuan dynasty}} in {{ill|Mongolia|en|Mongolia|WD=Q711|lt=Mongolia}} and China, Zhēngyuè 22, 1294 *{{ill|Shunzhi Emperor|en|Shunzhi Emperor|WD=Q310453|lt=Shunzhi Emperor}}, the third emperor of the Qing dynasty and the first Qing emperor to rule over China, from 1644 to 1661, Zhēngyuè 30, 1638 *{{ill|Qianlong Emperor|en|Qianlong Emperor|WD=Q19133|lt=Qianlong Emperor}}, the sixth emperor of the [[:en:Manchu|Manchu]]-led Qing dynasty, and the fourth Qing emperor to rule over China proper, Zhēngyuè 3, 1799 *{{ill|Daoguang Emperor|en|Daoguang Emperor|WD=Q334351|lt=Daoguang Emperor}}, a Qing emperor, Zhēngyuè 14, 1850

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[cdo:Nùng-lĭk 1 nguŏk]][[en:Zhengyue]][[gan:正月]][[hak:Nùng-la̍k 1-ngie̍t]][[ja:1月 (旧暦)]][[ko:음력 1월]][[th:เดือนอ้าย]][[vi:Tháng giêng]][[wuu:正月]][[za:Ndwencieng]][[zh-min-nan:Kū-le̍k 1 goe̍h]][[zh-yue:正月]][[zh:正月]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[cdo:Nùng-lĭk 1 nguŏk]][[en:Zhengyue]][[gan:正月]][[hak:Nùng-la̍k 1-ngie̍t]][[ja:1月 (旧暦)]][[ko:음력 1월]][[th:เดือนอ้าย]][[vi:Tháng giêng]][[wuu:正月]][[za:Ndwencieng]][[zh-min-nan:Kū-le̍k 1 goe̍h]][[zh-yue:正月]][[zh:正月]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[cdo:Nùng-lĭk 1 nguŏk]][[en:Zhengyue]][[gan:正月]][[hak:Nùng-la̍k 1-ngie̍t]][[ja:1月 (旧暦)]][[ko:음력 1월]][[th:เดือนอ้าย]][[vi:Tháng giêng]][[wuu:正月]][[za:Ndwencieng]][[zh-min-nan:Kū-le̍k 1 goe̍h]][[zh-yue:正月]][[zh:正月]]
{{Lunisolar January|qq=}} ''''Zhēngyuè''''' is the first {{ill|Month|en|Month|WD=Q5151|lt=Month}} of the {{ill|Year|en|Year|WD=Q577|lt=Year}} in the {{ill|Chinese calendar|en|Chinese calendar|WD=Q134032|lt=Chinese calendar}}. In general, the Vernal Showers are in Zhēngyuè. The name comes from the annual {{ill|Morale|en|Morale|WD=Q1465318|lt=Morale}}-building [[:en:Li (Confucian)|rite]] ({{zh|c=正旦大朝|qq=}}) in {{ill|Ancient China|en|Ancient China|WD=Q630276|lt=Ancient China}}.
Zhēngyuè 1 is the {{ill|Day|en|Day|WD=Q573|lt=Day}} of the {{ill|Rooster|en|Rooster|WD=Q2216236|lt=Rooster}}; Zhēngyuè 2 is the day of the {{ill|Dog|en|Dog|WD=Q144|lt=Dog}}; Zhēngyuè 3 is the day of the {{ill|Pig|en|Pig|WD=Q787|lt=Pig}}; Zhēngyuè 4 is the day of the {{ill|Goat|en|Goat|WD=Q2934|lt=Goat}}; Zhēngyuè 5 is the day of the {{ill|Ox|en|Ox|WD=Q473194|lt=Ox}}; Zhēngyuè 6 is the day of the {{ill|Horse|en|Horse|WD=Q726|lt=Horse}}; Zhēngyuè 7 is the day of the {{ill|Human|en|Human|WD=Q5|lt=Human}}.
'''Lunisolar January''' (旧暦一月) is the first {{ill|Month|en|Month|WD=Q5151|lt=Month}} of the year in the {{ill|Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|ja|旧暦|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|11=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|qq=}} ({{ill|Lunisolar calendar|en|Lunisolar calendar|WD=Q194235|lt=Lunisolar calendar}}).
Under definitions predating the {{ill|Tenpō calendar|en|Tenpō calendar|WD=Q1066717|lt=Tenpō Calendar}}, January was defined as the month that includes {{ill|Yushui (solar term)|en|Yushui (solar term)|WD=Q1364713|lt=Yushui}}. In the {{ill|New Calendar|ja|新暦|1=new calendar|lt=New Calendar|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/New Calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/New Calendar (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q125314718}}, it corresponds approximately to late {{ill|January|en|January|WD=Q108|lt=January}} to early {{ill|March|en|March|WD=Q110|lt=March}}.
Another name for January is '''Mutsuki''' (睦月). The etymology is explained at [[#Etymology of traditional Japanese name]]
In the Eastern lunisolar calendar, the number of days in a month, known as {{ill|Large and Small|ja|大小|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Large and Small|1=Large and Small|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10935019}}, (with large months having 30 days and small months 29 days) varies from year to year. As a result, there are years when January ends on the 29th, with no January 30th.
==Etymology of traditional Japanese name==
In {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}, the '''Lunar January''' is called '''Mutsuki''' (むつき), and today it is also used as an alternate name for January of the {{ill|Gregorian calendar|ja|新暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gregorian calendar|1=new calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q125314718}}. There are various theories about the origin of the name Mutsuki. The most widely accepted theory holds that it derives from “Mutsubizuki” (睦び月), meaning “month of harmony,” when all relatives gather to hold banquets. Other theories include “Mototsuki” (元つ月), “Moyutsuki” (萌月), and “Umutsuki” (生月).
In the Saru dialect of the {{ill|Ainu language|en|Ainu language|WD=Q27969|lt=Hokkaido Ainu language}}, it is called “Towetanne” (トウエタンネ; {{ill|Ainu language|en|Ainu language|WD=Q27969|lt=Ainu language}}: towetanne), meaning “the days become longer” (<ref>{{Cite web |title=アイヌ語カレンダー |url=https://www.ff-ainu.or.jp/calendardownload.html |website=(公益財団法人 アイヌ民族文化財団) |access-date=2025-01-12}}</ref>), and in the Asahikawa dialect, January is called “Inomi Cup” (イノミチュㇷ゚; {{ill|Ainu language|en|Ainu language|WD=Q27969|lt=Ainu language}}: inomi cup), meaning “month of praying to the gods” (<ref>{{Cite web |title=月刊シロㇿ 10月号（2015.10） |url=https://ainugo.nam.go.jp/siror/monthly/201510.html#01 |website=アイヌと自然 デジタル図鑑 |access-date=2025-01-09 |publisher={{ill|Ainu Museum|en|Ainu Museum|WD=Q10333378|lt=Ainu Museum}}}}</ref>). == Dates in Mutsuki == {| class="wikitable" |+ Dates in Mutsuki ! Year !! New Moon !! Last Day !! Number of Days |- | 2014 || {{ill|January 31|en|January 31|WD=Q2289|lt=January 31}} || {{ill|February 28|en|February 28|WD=Q2362|lt=February 28}} || 29 days |- | 2015 || {{ill|February 19|en|February 19|WD=Q2340|lt=February 19}} || {{ill|March 19|en|March 19|WD=Q2424|lt=March 19}} || 29 days |- | 2016 || {{ill|February 8|en|February 8|WD=Q2323|lt=February 8}} || {{ill|March 8|en|March 8|WD=Q2395|lt=March 8}} || 30 days |- | 2017 || {{ill|January 28|en|January 28|WD=Q2285|lt=January 28}} || {{ill|February 25|en|February 25|WD=Q2356|lt=February 25}} || 29 days |- | 2018 || {{ill|February 16|en|February 16|WD=Q2342|lt=February 16}} || {{ill|March 16|en|March 16|WD=Q2417|lt=March 16}} || 29 days |- | 2019 || {{ill|February 5|en|February 5|WD=Q2319|lt=February 5}} || {{ill|March 6|en|March 6|WD=Q2399|lt=March 6}} || 30 days |- | 2020 || {{ill|January 25|en|January 25|WD=Q2279|lt=January 25}} || {{ill|February 23|en|February 23|WD=Q2352|lt=February 23}} || 30 days |- | 2021 || {{ill|February 12|en|February 12|WD=Q2336|lt=February 12}} || {{ill|March 12|en|March 12|WD=Q2402|lt=March 12}} || 29 days |- | 2022 || {{ill|February 1|en|February 1|WD=Q2312|lt=February 1}} || {{ill|March 2|en|March 2|WD=Q2390|lt=March 2}} || 30 days |- | 2023 || {{ill|January 22|en|January 22|WD=Q2275|lt=January 22}} || {{ill|February 19|en|February 19|WD=Q2340|lt=February 19}} || 29 days |- | 2024 || {{ill|February 10|en|February 10|WD=Q2325|lt=February 10}} || {{ill|March 9|en|March 9|WD=Q2401|lt=March 9}} || 29 days |- | 2025 || {{ill|January 29|en|January 29|WD=Q2286|lt=January 29}} || {{ill|February 27|en|February 27|WD=Q2361|lt=February 27}} || 30 days |}

power in the final years of the dynasty, Zhēngyuè 23, 220 *{{ill|Emperor Xianzong of Tang|en|Emperor Xianzong of Tang|WD=Q9761|lt=Emperor Xianzong of Tang}}, an emperor of the Chinese Tang dynasty, dynasty}} of China, Zhēngyuè 7, 1067 *[[:en:Emperor Zhezong of Song|Emperor Zhezong of Song]], the seventh emperor of the Song dynasty of China, Zhēngyuè 12, 1100 *{{ill|Kublai Khan|en|Kublai Khan|WD=Q7523|lt=Kublai Khan}}, the fifth {{ill|Khagan|en|Khagan|WD=Q187878|lt=Khagan}} (Great Khan) of the Ikh Mongol Uls ({{ill|Mongol Empire|en|Mongol Empire|WD=Q12557|lt=Mongol Empire}}), {{ill|reign|en|reign|WD=Q7310094|lt=reign}}ing from 1260 to 1294, and the founder of the {{ill|Yuan dynasty|en|Yuan dynasty|WD=Q7313|lt=Yuan dynasty}} in {{ill|Mongolia|en|Mongolia|WD=Q711|lt=Mongolia}} and China, Zhēngyuè 22, 1294 *{{ill|Shunzhi Emperor|en|Shunzhi Emperor|WD=Q310453|lt=Shunzhi Emperor}}, the third emperor of the Qing dynasty and the first Qing emperor to rule over China, from 1644 to 1661, Zhēngyuè 30, 1638 *{{ill|Qianlong Emperor|en|Qianlong Emperor|WD=Q19133|lt=Qianlong Emperor}}, the sixth emperor of the [[:en:Manchu|Manchu]]-led Qing dynasty, and the fourth Qing emperor to rule over China proper, Zhēngyuè 3, 1799 *{{ill|Daoguang Emperor|en|Daoguang Emperor|WD=Q334351|lt=Daoguang Emperor}}, a Qing emperor, Zhēngyuè 14, 1850

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[cdo:Nùng-lĭk 1 nguŏk]][[en:Zhengyue]][[gan:正月]][[hak:Nùng-la̍k 1-ngie̍t]][[ja:1月 (旧暦)]][[ko:음력 1월]][[th:เดือนอ้าย]][[vi:Tháng giêng]][[wuu:正月]][[za:Ndwencieng]][[zh-min-nan:Kū-le̍k 1 goe̍h]][[zh-yue:正月]][[zh:正月]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[cdo:Nùng-lĭk 1 nguŏk]][[en:Zhengyue]][[gan:正月]][[hak:Nùng-la̍k 1-ngie̍t]][[ja:1月 (旧暦)]][[ko:음력 1월]][[th:เดือนอ้าย]][[vi:Tháng giêng]][[wuu:正月]][[za:Ndwencieng]][[zh-min-nan:Kū-le̍k 1 goe̍h]][[zh-yue:正月]][[zh:正月]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[cdo:Nùng-lĭk 1 nguŏk]][[en:Zhengyue]][[gan:正月]][[hak:Nùng-la̍k 1-ngie̍t]][[ja:1月 (旧暦)]][[ko:음력 1월]][[th:เดือนอ้าย]][[vi:Tháng giêng]][[wuu:正月]][[za:Ndwencieng]][[zh-min-nan:Kū-le̍k 1 goe̍h]][[zh-yue:正月]][[zh:正月]]
{{Lunisolar January|qq=}} ''''Zhēngyuè''''' is the first {{ill|Month|en|Month|WD=Q5151|lt=Month}} of the {{ill|Year|en|Year|WD=Q577|lt=Year}} in the {{ill|Chinese calendar|en|Chinese calendar|WD=Q134032|lt=Chinese calendar}}. In general, the Vernal Showers are in Zhēngyuè. The name comes from the annual {{ill|Morale|en|Morale|WD=Q1465318|lt=Morale}}-building [[:en:Li (Confucian)|rite]] ({{zh|c=正旦大朝|qq=}}) in {{ill|Ancient China|en|Ancient China|WD=Q630276|lt=Ancient China}}.
Zhēngyuè 1 is the {{ill|Day|en|Day|WD=Q573|lt=Day}} of the {{ill|Rooster|en|Rooster|WD=Q2216236|lt=Rooster}}; Zhēngyuè 2 is the day of the {{ill|Dog|en|Dog|WD=Q144|lt=Dog}}; Zhēngyuè 3 is the day of the {{ill|Pig|en|Pig|WD=Q787|lt=Pig}}; Zhēngyuè 4 is the day of the {{ill|Goat|en|Goat|WD=Q2934|lt=Goat}}; Zhēngyuè 5 is the day of the {{ill|Ox|en|Ox|WD=Q473194|lt=Ox}}; Zhēngyuè 6 is the day of the {{ill|Horse|en|Horse|WD=Q726|lt=Horse}}; Zhēngyuè 7 is the day of the {{ill|Human|en|Human|WD=Q5|lt=Human}}.
'''Lunisolar January''' (旧暦一月) is the first {{ill|Month|en|Month|WD=Q5151|lt=Month}} of the year in the {{ill|Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|ja|旧暦|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|11=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|qq=}} ({{ill|Lunisolar calendar|en|Lunisolar calendar|WD=Q194235|lt=Lunisolar calendar}}).
Under definitions predating the {{ill|Tenpō calendar|en|Tenpō calendar|WD=Q1066717|lt=Tenpō Calendar}}, January was defined as the month that includes {{ill|Yushui (solar term)|en|Yushui (solar term)|WD=Q1364713|lt=Yushui}}. In the {{ill|New Calendar|ja|新暦|1=new calendar|lt=New Calendar|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/New Calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/New Calendar (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q125314718}}, it corresponds approximately to late {{ill|January|en|January|WD=Q108|lt=January}} to early {{ill|March|en|March|WD=Q110|lt=March}}.
Another name for January is '''Mutsuki''' (睦月). The etymology is explained at [[#Etymology of traditional Japanese name]]
In the Eastern lunisolar calendar, the number of days in a month, known as {{ill|Large and Small|ja|大小|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Large and Small|1=Large and Small|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10935019}}, (with large months having 30 days and small months 29 days) varies from year to year. As a result, there are years when January ends on the 29th, with no January 30th.
==Etymology of traditional Japanese name==
In {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}, the '''Lunar January''' is called '''Mutsuki''' (むつき), and today it is also used as an alternate name for January of the {{ill|Gregorian calendar|ja|新暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gregorian calendar|1=new calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q125314718}}. There are various theories about the origin of the name Mutsuki. The most widely accepted theory holds that it derives from “Mutsubizuki” (睦び月), meaning “month of harmony,” when all relatives gather to hold banquets. Other theories include “Mototsuki” (元つ月), “Moyutsuki” (萌月), and “Umutsuki” (生月).
In the Saru dialect of the {{ill|Ainu language|en|Ainu language|WD=Q27969|lt=Hokkaido Ainu language}}, it is called “Towetanne” (トウエタンネ; {{ill|Ainu language|en|Ainu language|WD=Q27969|lt=Ainu language}}: towetanne), meaning “the days become longer” (<ref>{{Cite web |title=アイヌ語カレンダー |url=https://www.ff-ainu.or.jp/calendardownload.html |website=(公益財団法人 アイヌ民族文化財団) |access-date=2025-01-12}}</ref>), and in the Asahikawa dialect, January is called “Inomi Cup” (イノミチュㇷ゚; {{ill|Ainu language|en|Ainu language|WD=Q27969|lt=Ainu language}}: inomi cup), meaning “month of praying to the gods” (<ref>{{Cite web |title=月刊シロㇿ 10月号（2015.10） |url=https://ainugo.nam.go.jp/siror/monthly/201510.html#01 |website=アイヌと自然 デジタル図鑑 |access-date=2025-01-09 |publisher={{ill|Ainu Museum|en|Ainu Museum|WD=Q10333378|lt=Ainu Museum}}}}</ref>). == Dates in Mutsuki == {| class="wikitable" |+ Dates in Mutsuki ! Year !! New Moon !! Last Day !! Number of Days |- | 2014 || {{ill|January 31|en|January 31|WD=Q2289|lt=January 31}} || {{ill|February 28|en|February 28|WD=Q2362|lt=February 28}} || 29 days |- | 2015 || {{ill|February 19|en|February 19|WD=Q2340|lt=February 19}} || {{ill|March 19|en|March 19|WD=Q2424|lt=March 19}} || 29 days |- | 2016 || {{ill|February 8|en|February 8|WD=Q2323|lt=February 8}} || {{ill|March 8|en|March 8|WD=Q2395|lt=March 8}} || 30 days |- | 2017 || {{ill|January 28|en|January 28|WD=Q2285|lt=January 28}} || {{ill|February 25|en|February 25|WD=Q2356|lt=February 25}} || 29 days |- | 2018 || {{ill|February 16|en|February 16|WD=Q2342|lt=February 16}} || {{ill|March 16|en|March 16|WD=Q2417|lt=March 16}} || 29 days |- | 2019 || {{ill|February 5|en|February 5|WD=Q2319|lt=February 5}} || {{ill|March 6|en|March 6|WD=Q2399|lt=March 6}} || 30 days |- | 2020 || {{ill|January 25|en|January 25|WD=Q2279|lt=January 25}} || {{ill|February 23|en|February 23|WD=Q2352|lt=February 23}} || 30 days |- | 2021 || {{ill|February 12|en|February 12|WD=Q2336|lt=February 12}} || {{ill|March 12|en|March 12|WD=Q2402|lt=March 12}} || 29 days |- | 2022 || {{ill|February 1|en|February 1|WD=Q2312|lt=February 1}} || {{ill|March 2|en|March 2|WD=Q2390|lt=March 2}} || 30 days |- | 2023 || {{ill|January 22|en|January 22|WD=Q2275|lt=January 22}} || {{ill|February 19|en|February 19|WD=Q2340|lt=February 19}} || 29 days |- | 2024 || {{ill|February 10|en|February 10|WD=Q2325|lt=February 10}} || {{ill|March 9|en|March 9|WD=Q2401|lt=March 9}} || 29 days |- | 2025 || {{ill|January 29|en|January 29|WD=Q2286|lt=January 29}} || {{ill|February 27|en|February 27|WD=Q2361|lt=February 27}} || 30 days |}
== Overview == {{unreferenced|date=January 2015|qq=}} {{Italic title|reason=|qq=}}
==Festivals== *{{ill|Chinese New Year|en|Chinese New Year|WD=Q131772|lt=New Year's Day}} ({{zh|c=春节|qq=}}) is Zhēngyuè 1. The following New Year's Day is January 29, 2025, which is a statutory [[holiday]]. *The [[:en:Yuanxiao Festival|Yuanxiao Festival]] ({{zh|c=元宵|qq=}}) is Zhēngyuè 15. The following Yuanxiao is February 12, 2025.
==Birth== *{{ill|Wu Zetian|en|Wu Zetian|WD=Q9738|lt=Wu Zetian}}, the only {{ill|Female|en|Female|WD=Q43445|lt=Female}} {{ill|Emperor|en|Emperor|WD=Q39018|lt=Emperor}} of {{ill|China|en|China|WD=Q148|lt=China}}, Zhēngyuè 23, 624 *{{ill|en:Li Bai|en|en:Li Bai|WD=Q7071|lt=en:Li Bai}}, a Chinese {{ill|Poet|en|Poet|WD=Q49757|lt=Poet}} acclaimed to this day as a {{ill|genius|en|genius|WD=Q185030|lt=genius}} and romantic figure who took traditional {{ill|Poetry|en|Poetry|WD=Q482|lt=poetic}} forms to new heights, Zhēngyuè 16, 701 *{{ill|Emperor Shunzong of Tang|en|Emperor Shunzong of Tang|WD=Q9760|lt=Emperor Shunzong of Tang}}, an emperor of the Chinese {{ill|Tang dynasty|en|Tang dynasty|WD=Q9683|lt=Tang dynasty}}, Zhēngyuè 12, 761 *{{ill|Bai Juyi|en|Bai Juyi|WD=Q313324|lt=Bai Juyi}}, a Chinese {{ill|Poet|en|Poet|WD=Q49757|lt=Poet}} of the Tang dynasty, Zhēngyuè 20, 772 *{{ill|Emperor Xizong of Jin|en|Emperor Xizong of Jin|WD=Q5071|lt=Emperor Xizong of Jin}}, an emperor of the {{ill|Jin dynasty (1115–1234)|en|Jin dynasty (1115–1234)|WD=Q5066|lt=Jin dynasty}} which controlled northern China from 1115 to 1234, Zhēngyuè 17, 1119 *{{ill|Qiu Chuji|en|Qiu Chuji|WD=Q565148|lt=Qiu Chuji}}, a [[:en:Daoist|Daoist]] of {{ill|Wang Chongyang|en|Wang Chongyang|WD=Q701186|lt=Wang Chongyang}}, Zhēngyuè 9, 1148 *{{ill|Itō Suketaka|en|Itō Suketaka|WD=Q8014919|lt=Itō Suketaka}}, a samurai, daimyō and twelfth family leader of the Itō clan, Zhēngyuè 15, 1559 *{{ill|Li Hongzhang|en|Li Hongzhang|WD=Q378517|lt=Li Hongzhang}}, a {{ill|Politician|en|Politician|WD=Q82955|lt=Politician}}, {{ill|General|en|General|WD=Q83460|lt=General}}, and {{ill|Diplomat|en|Diplomat|WD=Q193391|lt=Diplomat}} of the late [[:en:Qing Empire|Qing Empire]], Zhēngyuè 5, 1823 *[[:en:Aisin-Gioro Puyi|Aisin-Gioro Puyi]], the last emperor of China and the twelfth and final ruler of the {{ill|Qing dynasty|en|Qing dynasty|WD=Q8733|lt=Qing dynasty}}, Zhēngyuè 14, 1906
==Death== *{{ill|Sun Jian|en|Sun Jian|WD=Q334101|lt=Sun Jian}}, a military general and {{ill|Warlord|en|Warlord|WD=Q220098|lt=Warlord}} who lived in the late [[:en:Eastern Han dynasty|Eastern Han dynasty]], Zhēngyuè 7, 191 *{{ill|Guan Yu|en|Guan Yu|WD=Q222797|lt=Guan Yu}}, a general serving under the warlord {{ill|Liu Bei|en|Liu Bei|WD=Q245315|lt=Liu Bei}} in the late Eastern Han dynasty, Zhēngyuè 13, 220 *{{ill|Cao Cao|en|Cao Cao|WD=Q204077|lt=Cao Cao}}, a warlord and the penultimate {{ill|Chancellor|en|Chancellor|WD=Q373085|lt=Chancellor}} of the Eastern Han dynasty who rose to great power in the final years of the dynasty, Zhēngyuè 23, 220 *{{ill|Emperor Xianzong of Tang|en|Emperor Xianzong of Tang|WD=Q9761|lt=Emperor Xianzong of Tang}}, an emperor of the Chinese Tang dynasty, Zhēngyuè 27, 820 *{{ill|Emperor Yingzong of Song|en|Emperor Yingzong of Song|WD=Q7479|lt=Emperor Yingzong of Song}}, the fifth emperor of the {{ill|Song dynasty|en|Song dynasty|WD=Q7462|lt=Song dynasty}} of China, Zhēngyuè 7, 1067 *[[:en:Emperor Zhezong of Song|Emperor Zhezong of Song]], the seventh emperor of the Song dynasty of China, Zhēngyuè 12, 1100 *{{ill|Kublai Khan|en|Kublai Khan|WD=Q7523|lt=Kublai Khan}}, the fifth {{ill|Khagan|en|Khagan|WD=Q187878|lt=Khagan}} (Great Khan) of the Ikh Mongol Uls ({{ill|Mongol Empire|en|Mongol Empire|WD=Q12557|lt=Mongol Empire}}), {{ill|reign|en|reign|WD=Q7310094|lt=reign}}ing from 1260 to 1294, and the founder of the {{ill|Yuan dynasty|en|Yuan dynasty|WD=Q7313|lt=Yuan dynasty}} in {{ill|Mongolia|en|Mongolia|WD=Q711|lt=Mongolia}} and China, Zhēngyuè 22, 1294 *{{ill|Shunzhi Emperor|en|Shunzhi Emperor|WD=Q310453|lt=Shunzhi Emperor}}, the third emperor of the Qing dynasty and the first Qing emperor to rule over China, from 1644 to 1661, Zhēngyuè 30, 1638 *{{ill|Qianlong Emperor|en|Qianlong Emperor|WD=Q19133|lt=Qianlong Emperor}}, the sixth emperor of the [[:en:Manchu|Manchu]]-led Qing dynasty, and the fourth Qing emperor to rule over China proper, Zhēngyuè 3, 1799 *{{ill|Daoguang Emperor|en|Daoguang Emperor|WD=Q334351|lt=Daoguang Emperor}}, a Qing emperor, Zhēngyuè 14, 1850

{{Lunisolar January|qq=}} ''''Zhēngyuè''''' is the first {{ill|Month|en|Month|WD=Q5151|lt=Month}} of the {{ill|Year|en|Year|WD=Q577|lt=Year}} in the {{ill|Chinese calendar|en|Chinese calendar|WD=Q134032|lt=Chinese calendar}}. In general, the Vernal Showers are in Zhēngyuè. The name comes from the annual {{ill|Morale|en|Morale|WD=Q1465318|lt=Morale}}-building [[:en:Li (Confucian)|rite]] ({{zh|c=正旦大朝|qq=}}) in {{ill|Ancient China|en|Ancient China|WD=Q630276|lt=Ancient China}}.
Zhēngyuè 1 is the {{ill|Day|en|Day|WD=Q573|lt=Day}} of the {{ill|Rooster|en|Rooster|WD=Q2216236|lt=Rooster}}; Zhēngyuè 2 is the day of the {{ill|Dog|en|Dog|WD=Q144|lt=Dog}}; Zhēngyuè 3 is the day of the {{ill|Pig|en|Pig|WD=Q787|lt=Pig}}; Zhēngyuè 4 is the day of the {{ill|Goat|en|Goat|WD=Q2934|lt=Goat}}; Zhēngyuè 5 is the day of the {{ill|Ox|en|Ox|WD=Q473194|lt=Ox}}; Zhēngyuè 6 is the day of the {{ill|Horse|en|Horse|WD=Q726|lt=Horse}}; Zhēngyuè 7 is the day of the {{ill|Human|en|Human|WD=Q5|lt=Human}}.
'''Lunisolar January''' (旧暦一月) is the first {{ill|Month|en|Month|WD=Q5151|lt=Month}} of the year in the {{ill|Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|ja|旧暦|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|11=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|qq=}} ({{ill|Lunisolar calendar|en|Lunisolar calendar|WD=Q194235|lt=Lunisolar calendar}}).
Under definitions predating the {{ill|Tenpō calendar|en|Tenpō calendar|WD=Q1066717|lt=Tenpō Calendar}}, January was defined as the month that includes {{ill|Yushui (solar term)|en|Yushui (solar term)|WD=Q1364713|lt=Yushui}}. In the {{ill|New Calendar|ja|新暦|1=new calendar|lt=New Calendar|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/New Calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/New Calendar (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q125314718}}, it corresponds approximately to late {{ill|January|en|January|WD=Q108|lt=January}} to early {{ill|March|en|March|WD=Q110|lt=March}}.
Another name for January is '''Mutsuki''' (睦月). The etymology is explained at [[#Etymology of traditional Japanese name]]
In the Eastern lunisolar calendar, the number of days in a month, known as {{ill|Large and Small|ja|大小|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Large and Small|1=Large and Small|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10935019}}, (with large months having 30 days and small months 29 days) varies from year to year. As a result, there are years when January ends on the 29th, with no January 30th.
==Etymology of traditional Japanese name==
In {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}, the '''Lunar January''' is called '''Mutsuki''' (むつき), and today it is also used as an alternate name for January of the {{ill|Gregorian calendar|ja|新暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gregorian calendar|1=new calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q125314718}}. There are various theories about the origin of the name Mutsuki. The most widely accepted theory holds that it derives from “Mutsubizuki” (睦び月), meaning “month of harmony,” when all relatives gather to hold banquets. Other theories include “Mototsuki” (元つ月), “Moyutsuki” (萌月), and “Umutsuki” (生月).
In the Saru dialect of the {{ill|Ainu language|en|Ainu language|WD=Q27969|lt=Hokkaido Ainu language}}, it is called “Towetanne” (トウエタンネ; {{ill|Ainu language|en|Ainu language|WD=Q27969|lt=Ainu language}}: towetanne), meaning “the days become longer” (<ref>{{Cite web |title=アイヌ語カレンダー |url=https://www.ff-ainu.or.jp/calendardownload.html |website=(公益財団法人 アイヌ民族文化財団) |access-date=2025-01-12}}</ref>), and in the Asahikawa dialect, January is called “Inomi Cup” (イノミチュㇷ゚; {{ill|Ainu language|en|Ainu language|WD=Q27969|lt=Ainu language}}: inomi cup), meaning “month of praying to the gods” (<ref>{{Cite web |title=月刊シロㇿ 10月号（2015.10） |url=https://ainugo.nam.go.jp/siror/monthly/201510.html#01 |website=アイヌと自然 デジタル図鑑 |access-date=2025-01-09 |publisher={{ill|Ainu Museum|en|Ainu Museum|WD=Q10333378|lt=Ainu Museum}}}}</ref>). == Dates in Mutsuki == {| class="wikitable" |+ Dates in Mutsuki ! The following Yuanxiao is February 12, 2025.

{{Lunisolar January|qq=}} ''''Zhēngyuè''''' is the first {{ill|Month|en|Month|WD=Q5151|lt=Month}} of the {{ill|Year|en|Year|WD=Q577|lt=Year}} in the {{ill|Chinese calendar|en|Chinese calendar|WD=Q134032|lt=Chinese calendar}}. In general, the Vernal Showers are in Zhēngyuè. The name comes from the annual {{ill|Morale|en|Morale|WD=Q1465318|lt=Morale}}-building [[:en:Li (Confucian)|rite]] ({{zh|c=正旦大朝|qq=}}) in {{ill|Ancient China|en|Ancient China|WD=Q630276|lt=Ancient China}}.
Zhēngyuè 1 is the {{ill|Day|en|Day|WD=Q573|lt=Day}} of the {{ill|Rooster|en|Rooster|WD=Q2216236|lt=Rooster}}; Zhēngyuè 2 is the day of the {{ill|Dog|en|Dog|WD=Q144|lt=Dog}}; Zhēngyuè 3 is the day of the {{ill|Pig|en|Pig|WD=Q787|lt=Pig}}; Zhēngyuè 4 is the day of the {{ill|Goat|en|Goat|WD=Q2934|lt=Goat}}; Zhēngyuè 5 is the day of the {{ill|Ox|en|Ox|WD=Q473194|lt=Ox}}; Zhēngyuè 6 is the day of the {{ill|Horse|en|Horse|WD=Q726|lt=Horse}}; Zhēngyuè 7 is the day of the {{ill|Human|en|Human|WD=Q5|lt=Human}}.
'''Lunisolar January''' (旧暦一月) is the first {{ill|Month|en|Month|WD=Q5151|lt=Month}} of the year in the {{ill|Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|ja|旧暦|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|11=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|qq=}} ({{ill|Lunisolar calendar|en|Lunisolar calendar|WD=Q194235|lt=Lunisolar calendar}}).
Under definitions predating the {{ill|Tenpō calendar|en|Tenpō calendar|WD=Q1066717|lt=Tenpō Calendar}}, January was defined as the month that includes {{ill|Yushui (solar term)|en|Yushui (solar term)|WD=Q1364713|lt=Yushui}}. In the {{ill|New Calendar|ja|新暦|1=new calendar|lt=New Calendar|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/New Calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/New Calendar (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q125314718}}, it corresponds approximately to late {{ill|January|en|January|WD=Q108|lt=January}} to early {{ill|March|en|March|WD=Q110|lt=March}}.
Another name for January is '''Mutsuki''' (睦月). The etymology is explained at [[#Etymology of traditional Japanese name]]
In the Eastern lunisolar calendar, the number of days in a month, known as {{ill|Large and Small|ja|大小|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Large and Small|1=Large and Small|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10935019}}, (with large months having 30 days and small months 29 days) varies from year to year. As a result, there are years when January ends on the 29th, with no January 30th.
==Etymology of traditional Japanese name==
In {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}, the '''Lunar January''' is called '''Mutsuki''' (むつき), and today it is also used as an alternate name for January of the {{ill|Gregorian calendar|ja|新暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gregorian calendar|1=new calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q125314718}}. There are various theories about the origin of the name Mutsuki. The most widely accepted theory holds that it derives from “Mutsubizuki” (睦び月), meaning “month of harmony,” when all relatives gather to hold banquets. Other theories include “Mototsuki” (元つ月), “Moyutsuki” (萌月), and “Umutsuki” (生月).
In the Saru dialect of the {{ill|Ainu language|en|Ainu language|WD=Q27969|lt=Hokkaido Ainu language}}, it is called “Towetanne” (トウエタンネ; {{ill|Ainu language|en|Ainu language|WD=Q27969|lt=Ainu language}}: towetanne), meaning “the days become longer” (<ref>{{Cite web |title=アイヌ語カレンダー |url=https://www.ff-ainu.or.jp/calendardownload.html |website=(公益財団法人 アイヌ民族文化財団) |access-date=2025-01-12}}</ref>), and in the Asahikawa dialect, January is called “Inomi Cup” (イノミチュㇷ゚; {{ill|Ainu language|en|Ainu language|WD=Q27969|lt=Ainu language}}: inomi cup), meaning “month of praying to the gods” (<ref>{{Cite web |title=月刊シロㇿ 10月号（2015.10） |url=https://ainugo.nam.go.jp/siror/monthly/201510.html#01 |website=アイヌと自然 デジタル図鑑 |access-date=2025-01-09 |publisher={{ill|Ainu Museum|en|Ainu Museum|WD=Q10333378|lt=Ainu Museum}}}}</ref>). == Dates in Mutsuki == {| class="wikitable" |+ Dates in Mutsuki ! {{ill|reign|en|reign|WD=Q7310094|lt=reign}}ing from 1260 to 1294, and the founder of the {{ill|Yuan dynasty|en|Yuan dynasty|WD=Q7313|lt=Yuan dynasty}} in {{ill|Mongolia|en|Mongolia|WD=Q711|lt=Mongolia}} and China, Zhēngyuè 22, 1294 *{{ill|Shunzhi Emperor|en|Shunzhi Emperor|WD=Q310453|lt=Shunzhi Emperor}}, the third emperor of the Qing dynasty and the first Qing emperor to rule over China, from 1644 to 1661, Zhēngyuè 30, 1638 *{{ill|Qianlong Emperor|en|Qianlong Emperor|WD=Q19133|lt=Qianlong Emperor}}, the sixth emperor of the [[:en:Manchu|Manchu]]-led Qing dynasty, and the fourth Qing emperor to rule over China proper, Zhēngyuè 3, 1799 *{{ill|Daoguang Emperor|en|Daoguang Emperor|WD=Q334351|lt=Daoguang Emperor}}, a Qing emperor, Zhēngyuè 14, 1850

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[cdo:Nùng-lĭk 1 nguŏk]][[en:Zhengyue]][[gan:正月]][[hak:Nùng-la̍k 1-ngie̍t]][[ja:1月 (旧暦)]][[ko:음력 1월]][[th:เดือนอ้าย]][[vi:Tháng giêng]][[wuu:正月]][[za:Ndwencieng]][[zh-min-nan:Kū-le̍k 1 goe̍h]][[zh-yue:正月]][[zh:正月]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[cdo:Nùng-lĭk 1 nguŏk]][[en:Zhengyue]][[gan:正月]][[hak:Nùng-la̍k 1-ngie̍t]][[ja:1月 (旧暦)]][[ko:음력 1월]][[th:เดือนอ้าย]][[vi:Tháng giêng]][[wuu:正月]][[za:Ndwencieng]][[zh-min-nan:Kū-le̍k 1 goe̍h]][[zh-yue:正月]][[zh:正月]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[cdo:Nùng-lĭk 1 nguŏk]][[en:Zhengyue]][[gan:正月]][[hak:Nùng-la̍k 1-ngie̍t]][[ja:1月 (旧暦)]][[ko:음력 1월]][[th:เดือนอ้าย]][[vi:Tháng giêng]][[wuu:正月]][[za:Ndwencieng]][[zh-min-nan:Kū-le̍k 1 goe̍h]][[zh-yue:正月]][[zh:正月]]
{{Lunisolar January|qq=}} ''''Zhēngyuè''''' is the first {{ill|Month|en|Month|WD=Q5151|lt=Month|1=Month}} of the {{ill|Year|en|Year|WD=Q577|lt=Year|1=Year}} in the {{ill|Chinese calendar|en|Chinese calendar|WD=Q134032|lt=Chinese calendar|1=Chinese calendar}}. In general, the Vernal Showers are in Zhēngyuè. The name comes from the annual {{ill|Morale|en|Morale|WD=Q1465318|lt=Morale|1=Morale}}-building [[:en:Li (Confucian)|rite]] ({{zh|c=正旦大朝|qq=}}) in {{ill|Ancient China|en|Ancient China|WD=Q630276|lt=Ancient China|1=Ancient China}}.
Zhēngyuè 1 is the {{ill|Day|en|Day|WD=Q573|lt=Day|1=Day}} of the {{ill|Rooster|en|Rooster|WD=Q2216236|lt=Rooster|1=Rooster}}; Zhēngyuè 2 is the day of the {{ill|Dog|en|Dog|WD=Q144|lt=Dog|1=Dog}}; Zhēngyuè 3 is the day of the {{ill|Pig|en|Pig|WD=Q787|lt=Pig|1=Pig}}; Zhēngyuè 4 is the day of the {{ill|Goat|en|Goat|WD=Q2934|lt=Goat|1=Goat}}; Zhēngyuè 5 is the day of the {{ill|Ox|en|Ox|WD=Q473194|lt=Ox|1=Ox}}; Zhēngyuè 6 is the day of the {{ill|Horse|en|Horse|WD=Q726|lt=Horse|1=Horse}}; Zhēngyuè 7 is the day of the {{ill|Human|en|Human|WD=Q5|lt=Human|1=Human}}.
'''Lunisolar January''' (旧暦一月) is the first {{ill|Month|en|Month|WD=Q5151|lt=Month|1=Month}} of the year in the {{ill|Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|ja|旧暦|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|11=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|qq=}} ({{ill|Lunisolar calendar|en|Lunisolar calendar|WD=Q194235|lt=Lunisolar calendar|1=Lunisolar calendar}}).
Under definitions predating the {{ill|Tenpō calendar|en|Tenpō calendar|WD=Q1066717|lt=Tenpō Calendar|1=Tenpō calendar}}, January was defined as the month that includes {{ill|Yushui (solar term)|en|Yushui (solar term)|WD=Q1364713|lt=Yushui|1=Yushui (solar term)}}. In the {{ill|New Calendar|ja|新暦|1=The new calendar|lt=New Calendar|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/New Calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/New Calendar (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q125314718}}, it corresponds approximately to late {{ill|January|en|January|WD=Q108|lt=January|1=January}} to early {{ill|March|en|March|WD=Q110|lt=March|1=March}}.
Another name for January is '''Mutsuki''' (睦月). The etymology is explained at [[#Etymology of traditional Japanese name]]
In the Eastern lunisolar calendar, the number of days in a month, known as {{ill|Large and Small|ja|大小|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Large and Small|1=Large and Small|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10935019}}, (with large months having 30 days and small months 29 days) varies from year to year. As a result, there are years when January ends on the 29th, with no January 30th.
==Etymology of traditional Japanese name==
In {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}, the '''Lunar January''' is called '''Mutsuki''' (むつき), and today it is also used as an alternate name for January of the {{ill|Gregorian calendar|ja|新暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gregorian calendar|1=The new calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q125314718}}. There are various theories about the origin of the name Mutsuki. The most widely accepted theory holds that it derives from “Mutsubizuki” (睦び月), meaning “month of harmony,” when all relatives gather to hold banquets. Other theories include “Mototsuki” (元つ月), “Moyutsuki” (萌月), and “Umutsuki” (生月).
In the Saru dialect of the {{ill|Ainu language|en|Ainu language|WD=Q27969|lt=Hokkaido Ainu language|1=Ainu language}}, it is called “Towetanne” (トウエタンネ; {{ill|Ainu language|en|Ainu language|WD=Q27969|lt=Ainu language|1=Ainu language}}: towetanne), meaning “the days become longer” (<ref>{{Cite web |title=アイヌ語カレンダー |url=https://www.ff-ainu.or.jp/calendardownload.html |website=(公益財団法人 アイヌ民族文化財団) |access-date=2025-01-12}}</ref>), and in the Asahikawa dialect, January is called “Inomi Cup” (イノミチュㇷ゚; {{ill|Ainu language|en|Ainu language|WD=Q27969|lt=Ainu language|1=Ainu language}}: inomi cup), meaning “month of praying to the gods” (<ref>{{Cite web |title=月刊シロㇿ 10月号（2015.10） |url=https://ainugo.nam.go.jp/siror/monthly/201510.html#01 |website=アイヌと自然 デジタル図鑑 |access-date=2025-01-09 |publisher={{ill|Ainu Museum|en|Ainu Museum|WD=Q10333378|lt=Ainu Museum|1=Ainu Museum}}}}</ref>). == Dates in Mutsuki == {| class="wikitable" |+ Dates in Mutsuki ! Year !! New Moon !! Last Day !! Number of Days |- | 2014 || {{ill|January 31|en|January 31|WD=Q2289|lt=January 31|1=January 31}} || {{ill|February 28|en|February 28|WD=Q2362|lt=February 28|1=February 28}} || 29 days |- | 2015 || {{ill|February 19|en|February 19|WD=Q2340|lt=February 19|1=February 19}} || {{ill|March 19|en|March 19|WD=Q2424|lt=March 19|1=March 19}} || 29 days |- | 2016 || {{ill|February 8|en|February 8|WD=Q2323|lt=February 8|1=February 8}} || {{ill|March 8|en|March 8|WD=Q2395|lt=March 8|1=March 8}} || 30 days |- | 2017 || {{ill|January 28|en|January 28|WD=Q2285|lt=January 28|1=January 28}} || {{ill|February 25|en|February 25|WD=Q2356|lt=February 25|1=February 25}} || 29 days |- | 2018 || {{ill|February 16|en|February 16|WD=Q2342|lt=February 16|1=February 16}} || {{ill|March 16|en|March 16|WD=Q2417|lt=March 16|1=March 16}} || 29 days |- | 2019 || {{ill|February 5|en|February 5|WD=Q2319|lt=February 5|1=February 5}} || {{ill|March 6|en|March 6|WD=Q2399|lt=March 6|1=March 6}} || 30 days |- | 2020 || {{ill|January 25|en|January 25|WD=Q2279|lt=January 25|1=January 25}} || {{ill|February 23|en|February 23|WD=Q2352|lt=February 23|1=February 23}} || 30 days |- | 2021 || {{ill|February 12|en|February 12|WD=Q2336|lt=February 12|1=February 12}} || {{ill|March 12|en|March 12|WD=Q2402|lt=March 12|1=March 12}} || 29 days |- | 2022 || {{ill|February 1|en|February 1|WD=Q2312|lt=February 1|1=February 1}} || {{ill|March 2|en|March 2|WD=Q2390|lt=March 2|1=March 2}} || 30 days |- | 2023 || {{ill|January 22|en|January 22|WD=Q2275|lt=January 22|1=January 22}} || {{ill|February 19|en|February 19|WD=Q2340|lt=February 19|1=February 19}} || 29 days |- | 2024 || {{ill|February 10|en|February 10|WD=Q2325|lt=February 10|1=February 10}} || {{ill|March 9|en|March 9|WD=Q2401|lt=March 9|1=March 9}} || 29 days |- | 2025 || {{ill|January 29|en|January 29|WD=Q2286|lt=January 29|1=January 29}} || {{ill|February 27|en|February 27|WD=Q2361|lt=February 27|1=February 27}} || 30 days |}
== Overview == {{unreferenced|date=January 2015|qq=}} {{Italic title|reason=|qq=}}
==Festivals== *{{ill|Chinese New Year|en|Chinese New Year|WD=Q131772|lt=New Year's Day|1=Chinese New Year}} ({{zh|c=春节|qq=}}) is Zhēngyuè 1. The following New Year's Day is January 29, 2025, which is a statutory [[holiday]]. *The [[:en:Yuanxiao Festival|Yuanxiao Festival]] ({{zh|c=元宵|qq=}}) is Zhēngyuè 15. The following Yuanxiao is February 12, 2025.
==Birth== *{{ill|Wu Zetian|en|Wu Zetian|WD=Q9738|lt=Wu Zetian|1=Wu Zetian}}, the only {{ill|Female|en|Female|WD=Q43445|lt=Female|1=Female}} {{ill|Emperor|en|Emperor|WD=Q39018|lt=Emperor|1=Emperor}} of {{ill|China|en|China|WD=Q148|lt=China|1=China}}, Zhēngyuè 23, 624 *{{ill|en:Li Bai|en|en:Li Bai|WD=Q7071|lt=en:Li Bai|1=Li Bai}}, a Chinese {{ill|Poet|en|Poet|WD=Q49757|lt=Poet|1=Poet}} acclaimed to this day as a {{ill|genius|en|genius|WD=Q185030|lt=genius|1=Genius}} and romantic figure who took traditional {{ill|Poetry|en|Poetry|WD=Q482|lt=poetic|1=Poetry}} forms to new heights, Zhēngyuè 16, 701 *{{ill|Emperor Shunzong of Tang|en|Emperor Shunzong of Tang|WD=Q9760|lt=Emperor Shunzong of Tang|1=Emperor Shunzong of Tang}}, an emperor of the Chinese {{ill|Tang dynasty|en|Tang dynasty|WD=Q9683|lt=Tang dynasty|1=Tang dynasty}}, Zhēngyuè 12, 761 *{{ill|Bai Juyi|en|Bai Juyi|WD=Q313324|lt=Bai Juyi|1=Bai Juyi}}, a Chinese {{ill|Poet|en|Poet|WD=Q49757|lt=Poet|1=Poet}} of the Tang dynasty, Zhēngyuè 20, 772 *{{ill|Emperor Xizong of Jin|en|Emperor Xizong of Jin|WD=Q5071|lt=Emperor Xizong of Jin|1=Emperor Xizong of Jin}}, an emperor of the {{ill|Jin dynasty (1115–1234)|en|Jin dynasty (1115–1234)|WD=Q5066|lt=Jin dynasty|1=Jin dynasty (1115–1234)}} which controlled northern China from 1115 to 1234, Zhēngyuè 17, 1119 *{{ill|Qiu Chuji|en|Qiu Chuji|WD=Q565148|lt=Qiu Chuji|1=Qiu Chuji}}, a [[:en:Daoist|Daoist]] of {{ill|Wang Chongyang|en|Wang Chongyang|WD=Q701186|lt=Wang Chongyang|1=Wang Chongyang}}, Zhēngyuè 9, 1148 *{{ill|Itō Suketaka|en|Itō Suketaka|WD=Q8014919|lt=Itō Suketaka|1=Itō Suketaka}}, a samurai, daimyō and twelfth family leader of the Itō clan, Zhēngyuè 15, 1559 *{{ill|Li Hongzhang|en|Li Hongzhang|WD=Q378517|lt=Li Hongzhang|1=Li Hongzhang}}, a {{ill|Politician|en|Politician|WD=Q82955|lt=Politician|1=Politician}}, {{ill|General|en|General|WD=Q83460|lt=General|1=General}}, and {{ill|Diplomat|en|Diplomat|WD=Q193391|lt=Diplomat|1=Diplomat}} of the late [[:en:Qing Empire|Qing Empire]], Zhēngyuè 5, 1823 *[[:en:Aisin-Gioro Puyi|Aisin-Gioro Puyi]], the last emperor of China and the twelfth and final ruler of the {{ill|Qing dynasty|en|Qing dynasty|WD=Q8733|lt=Qing dynasty|1=Qing dynasty}}, Zhēngyuè 14, 1906
==Death== *{{ill|Sun Jian|en|Sun Jian|WD=Q334101|lt=Sun Jian|1=Sun Jian}}, a military general and {{ill|Warlord|en|Warlord|WD=Q220098|lt=Warlord|1=Warlord}} who lived in the late [[:en:Eastern Han dynasty|Eastern Han dynasty]], Zhēngyuè 7, 191 *{{ill|Guan Yu|en|Guan Yu|WD=Q222797|lt=Guan Yu|1=Guan Yu}}, a general serving under the warlord {{ill|Liu Bei|en|Liu Bei|WD=Q245315|lt=Liu Bei|1=Liu Bei}} in the late Eastern Han dynasty, Zhēngyuè 13, 220 *{{ill|Cao Cao|en|Cao Cao|WD=Q204077|lt=Cao Cao|1=Cao Cao}}, a warlord and the penultimate {{ill|Chancellor|en|Chancellor|WD=Q373085|lt=Chancellor|1=Chancellor}} of the Eastern Han dynasty who rose to great power in the final years of the dynasty, Zhēngyuè 23, 220 *{{ill|Emperor Xianzong of Tang|en|Emperor Xianzong of Tang|WD=Q9761|lt=Emperor Xianzong of Tang|1=Emperor Xianzong of Tang}}, an emperor of the Chinese Tang dynasty, Zhēngyuè 27, 820 *{{ill|Emperor Yingzong of Song|en|Emperor Yingzong of Song|WD=Q7479|lt=Emperor Yingzong of Song|1=Emperor Yingzong of Song}}, the fifth emperor of the {{ill|Song dynasty|en|Song dynasty|WD=Q7462|lt=Song dynasty|1=Song dynasty}} of China, Zhēngyuè 7, 1067 *[[:en:Emperor Zhezong of Song|Emperor Zhezong of Song]], the seventh emperor of the Song dynasty of China, Zhēngyuè 12, 1100 *{{ill|Kublai Khan|en|Kublai Khan|WD=Q7523|lt=Kublai Khan|1=Kublai Khan}}, the fifth {{ill|Khagan|en|Khagan|WD=Q187878|lt=Khagan|1=Khagan}} (Great Khan) of the Ikh Mongol Uls ({{ill|Mongol Empire|en|Mongol Empire|WD=Q12557|lt=Mongol Empire|1=Mongol Empire}}), {{ill|reign|en|reign|WD=Q7310094|lt=reign|1=Reign}}ing from 1260 to 1294, and the founder of the {{ill|Yuan dynasty|en|Yuan dynasty|WD=Q7313|lt=Yuan dynasty|1=Yuan dynasty}} in {{ill|Mongolia|en|Mongolia|WD=Q711|lt=Mongolia|1=Mongolia}} and China, Zhēngyuè 22, 1294 *{{ill|Shunzhi Emperor|en|Shunzhi Emperor|WD=Q310453|lt=Shunzhi Emperor|1=Shunzhi Emperor}}, the third emperor of the Qing dynasty and the first Qing emperor to rule over China, from 1644 to 1661, Zhēngyuè 30, 1638 *{{ill|Qianlong Emperor|en|Qianlong Emperor|WD=Q19133|lt=Qianlong Emperor|1=Qianlong Emperor}}, the sixth emperor of the [[:en:Manchu|Manchu]]-led Qing dynasty, and the fourth Qing emperor to rule over China proper, Zhēngyuè 3, 1799 *{{ill|Daoguang Emperor|en|Daoguang Emperor|WD=Q334351|lt=Daoguang Emperor|1=Daoguang Emperor}}, a Qing emperor, Zhēngyuè 14, 1850

{{Lunisolar January|qq=}} ''''Zhēngyuè''''' is the first {{ill|Month|en|Month|WD=Q5151|lt=Month|1=Month}} of the {{ill|Year|en|Year|WD=Q577|lt=Year|1=Year}} in the {{ill|Chinese calendar|en|Chinese calendar|WD=Q134032|lt=Chinese calendar|1=Chinese calendar}}. In general, the Vernal Showers are in Zhēngyuè. The name comes from the annual {{ill|Morale|en|Morale|WD=Q1465318|lt=Morale|1=Morale}}-building [[:en:Li (Confucian)|rite]] ({{zh|c=正旦大朝|qq=}}) in {{ill|Ancient China|en|Ancient China|WD=Q630276|lt=Ancient China|1=Ancient China}}.
Zhēngyuè 1 is the {{ill|Day|en|Day|WD=Q573|lt=Day|1=Day}} of the {{ill|Rooster|en|Rooster|WD=Q2216236|lt=Rooster|1=Rooster}}; Zhēngyuè 2 is the day of the {{ill|Dog|en|Dog|WD=Q144|lt=Dog|1=Dog}}; Zhēngyuè 3 is the day of the {{ill|Pig|en|Pig|WD=Q787|lt=Pig|1=Pig}}; Zhēngyuè 4 is the day of the {{ill|Goat|en|Goat|WD=Q2934|lt=Goat|1=Goat}}; Zhēngyuè 5 is the day of the {{ill|Ox|en|Ox|WD=Q473194|lt=Ox|1=Ox}}; Zhēngyuè 6 is the day of the {{ill|Horse|en|Horse|WD=Q726|lt=Horse|1=Horse}}; Zhēngyuè 7 is the day of the {{ill|Human|en|Human|WD=Q5|lt=Human|1=Human}}.
'''Lunisolar January''' (旧暦一月) is the first {{ill|Month|en|Month|WD=Q5151|lt=Month|1=Month}} of the year in the {{ill|Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|ja|旧暦|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|11=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|qq=}} ({{ill|Lunisolar calendar|en|Lunisolar calendar|WD=Q194235|lt=Lunisolar calendar|1=Lunisolar calendar}}).
Under definitions predating the {{ill|Tenpō calendar|en|Tenpō calendar|WD=Q1066717|lt=Tenpō Calendar|1=Tenpō calendar}}, January was defined as the month that includes {{ill|Yushui (solar term)|en|Yushui (solar term)|WD=Q1364713|lt=Yushui|1=Yushui (solar term)}}. In the {{ill|New Calendar|ja|新暦|1=The new calendar|lt=New Calendar|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/New Calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/New Calendar (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q125314718}}, it corresponds approximately to late {{ill|January|en|January|WD=Q108|lt=January|1=January}} to early {{ill|March|en|March|WD=Q110|lt=March|1=March}}.
Another name for January is '''Mutsuki''' (睦月). The etymology is explained at [[#Etymology of traditional Japanese name]]
In the Eastern lunisolar calendar, the number of days in a month, known as {{ill|Large and Small|ja|大小|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Large and Small|1=Large and Small|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10935019}}, (with large months having 30 days and small months 29 days) varies from year to year. As a result, there are years when January ends on the 29th, with no January 30th.
==Etymology of traditional Japanese name==
In {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}, the '''Lunar January''' is called '''Mutsuki''' (むつき), and today it is also used as an alternate name for January of the {{ill|Gregorian calendar|ja|新暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gregorian calendar|1=The new calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q125314718}}. There are various theories about the origin of the name Mutsuki. The most widely accepted theory holds that it derives from “Mutsubizuki” (睦び月), meaning “month of harmony,” when all relatives gather to hold banquets. Other theories include “Mototsuki” (元つ月), “Moyutsuki” (萌月), and “Umutsuki” (生月).
In the Saru dialect of the {{ill|Ainu language|en|Ainu language|WD=Q27969|lt=Hokkaido Ainu language|1=Ainu language}}, it is called “Towetanne” (トウエタンネ; {{ill|Ainu language|en|Ainu language|WD=Q27969|lt=Ainu language|1=Ainu language}}: towetanne), meaning “the days become longer” (<ref>{{Cite web |title=アイヌ語カレンダー |url=https://www.ff-ainu.or.jp/calendardownload.html |website=(公益財団法人 アイヌ民族文化財団) |access-date=2025-01-12}}</ref>), and in the Asahikawa dialect, January is called “Inomi Cup” (イノミチュㇷ゚; {{ill|Ainu language|en|Ainu language|WD=Q27969|lt=Ainu language|1=Ainu language}}: inomi cup), meaning “month of praying to the gods” (<ref>{{Cite web |title=月刊シロㇿ 10月号（2015.10） |url=https://ainugo.nam.go.jp/siror/monthly/201510.html#01 |website=アイヌと自然 デジタル図鑑 |access-date=2025-01-09 |publisher={{ill|Ainu Museum|en|Ainu Museum|WD=Q10333378|lt=Ainu Museum|1=Ainu Museum}}}}</ref>). == Dates in Mutsuki == {| class="wikitable" |+ Dates in Mutsuki ! ==Festivals== *{{ill|Chinese New Year|en|Chinese New Year|WD=Q131772|lt=New Year's Day|1=Chinese New Year}} ({{zh|c=春节|qq=}}) is Zhēngyuè 1. The following New Year's Day is January 29, 2025, which is a statutory [[holiday]]. *The [[:en:Yuanxiao Festival|Yuanxiao Festival]] ({{zh|c=元宵|qq=}}) is Zhēngyuè 15. The following Yuanxiao is February 12, 2025.

{{Lunisolar January|qq=}} ''''Zhēngyuè''''' is the first {{ill|Month|en|Month|WD=Q5151|lt=Month|1=Month}} of the {{ill|Year|en|Year|WD=Q577|lt=Year|1=Year}} in the {{ill|Chinese calendar|en|Chinese calendar|WD=Q134032|lt=Chinese calendar|1=Chinese calendar}}. In general, the Vernal Showers are in Zhēngyuè. The name comes from the annual {{ill|Morale|en|Morale|WD=Q1465318|lt=Morale|1=Morale}}-building [[:en:Li (Confucian)|rite]] ({{zh|c=正旦大朝|qq=}}) in {{ill|Ancient China|en|Ancient China|WD=Q630276|lt=Ancient China|1=Ancient China}}.
Zhēngyuè 1 is the {{ill|Day|en|Day|WD=Q573|lt=Day|1=Day}} of the {{ill|Rooster|en|Rooster|WD=Q2216236|lt=Rooster|1=Rooster}}; Zhēngyuè 2 is the day of the {{ill|Dog|en|Dog|WD=Q144|lt=Dog|1=Dog}}; Zhēngyuè 3 is the day of the {{ill|Pig|en|Pig|WD=Q787|lt=Pig|1=Pig}}; Zhēngyuè 4 is the day of the {{ill|Goat|en|Goat|WD=Q2934|lt=Goat|1=Goat}}; Zhēngyuè 5 is the day of the {{ill|Ox|en|Ox|WD=Q473194|lt=Ox|1=Ox}}; Zhēngyuè 6 is the day of the {{ill|Horse|en|Horse|WD=Q726|lt=Horse|1=Horse}}; Zhēngyuè 7 is the day of the {{ill|Human|en|Human|WD=Q5|lt=Human|1=Human}}.
'''Lunisolar January''' (旧暦一月) is the first {{ill|Month|en|Month|WD=Q5151|lt=Month|1=Month}} of the year in the {{ill|Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|ja|旧暦|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|11=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|qq=}} ({{ill|Lunisolar calendar|en|Lunisolar calendar|WD=Q194235|lt=Lunisolar calendar|1=Lunisolar calendar}}).
Under definitions predating the {{ill|Tenpō calendar|en|Tenpō calendar|WD=Q1066717|lt=Tenpō Calendar|1=Tenpō calendar}}, January was defined as the month that includes {{ill|Yushui (solar term)|en|Yushui (solar term)|WD=Q1364713|lt=Yushui|1=Yushui (solar term)}}. In the {{ill|New Calendar|ja|新暦|1=The new calendar|lt=New Calendar|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/New Calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/New Calendar (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q125314718}}, it corresponds approximately to late {{ill|January|en|January|WD=Q108|lt=January|1=January}} to early {{ill|March|en|March|WD=Q110|lt=March|1=March}}.
Another name for January is '''Mutsuki''' (睦月). The etymology is explained at [[#Etymology of traditional Japanese name]]
In the Eastern lunisolar calendar, the number of days in a month, known as {{ill|Large and Small|ja|大小|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Large and Small|1=Large and Small|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10935019}}, (with large months having 30 days and small months 29 days) varies from year to year. As a result, there are years when January ends on the 29th, with no January 30th.

In {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}, the '''Lunar January''' is called '''Mutsuki''' (むつき), and today it is also used as an alternate name for January of the {{ill|Gregorian calendar|ja|新暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gregorian calendar|1=The new calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q125314718}}. There are various theories about the origin of the name Mutsuki. The most widely accepted theory holds that it derives from “Mutsubizuki” (睦び月), meaning “month of harmony,” when all relatives gather to hold banquets. Other theories include “Mototsuki” (元つ月), “Moyutsuki” (萌月), and “Umutsuki” (生月).
In the Saru dialect of the {{ill|Ainu language|en|Ainu language|WD=Q27969|lt=Hokkaido Ainu language|1=Ainu language}}, it is called “Towetanne” (トウエタンネ; {{ill|Ainu language|en|Ainu language|WD=Q27969|lt=Ainu language|1=Ainu language}}: towetanne), meaning “the days become longer” (<ref>{{Cite web |title=アイヌ語カレンダー |url=https://www.ff-ainu.or.jp/calendardownload.html |website=(公益財団法人 アイヌ民族文化財団) |access-date=2025-01-12}}</ref>), and in the Asahikawa dialect, January is called “Inomi Cup” (イノミチュㇷ゚; {{ill|Ainu language|en|Ainu language|WD=Q27969|lt=Ainu language|1=Ainu language}}: inomi cup), meaning “month of praying to the gods” (<ref>{{Cite web |title=月刊シロㇿ 10月号（2015.10） |url=https://ainugo.nam.go.jp/siror/monthly/201510.html#01 |website=アイヌと自然 デジタル図鑑 |access-date=2025-01-09 |publisher={{ill|Ainu Museum|en|Ainu Museum|WD=Q10333378|lt=Ainu Museum|1=Ainu Museum}}}}</ref>). == Dates in Mutsuki == {| class="wikitable" |+ Dates in Mutsuki ! ==Festivals== *{{ill|Chinese New Year|en|Chinese New Year|WD=Q131772|lt=New Year's Day|1=Chinese New Year}} ({{zh|c=春节|qq=}}) is Zhēngyuè 1. The following New Year's Day is January 29, 2025, which is a statutory [[holiday]]. *The [[:en:Yuanxiao Festival|Yuanxiao Festival]] ({{zh|c=元宵|qq=}}) is Zhēngyuè 15. The following Yuanxiao is February 12, 2025.

{{Lunisolar January|qq=}} ''''Zhēngyuè''''' is the first {{ill|Month|en|Month|WD=Q5151|lt=Month|1=Month}} of the {{ill|Year|en|Year|WD=Q577|lt=Year|1=Year}} in the {{ill|Chinese calendar|en|Chinese calendar|WD=Q134032|lt=Chinese calendar|1=Chinese calendar}}. In general, the Vernal Showers are in Zhēngyuè. The name comes from the annual {{ill|Morale|en|Morale|WD=Q1465318|lt=Morale|1=Morale}}-building [[:en:Li (Confucian)|rite]] ({{zh|c=正旦大朝|qq=}}) in {{ill|Ancient China|en|Ancient China|WD=Q630276|lt=Ancient China|1=Ancient China}}.
Zhēngyuè 1 is the {{ill|Day|en|Day|WD=Q573|lt=Day|1=Day}} of the {{ill|Rooster|en|Rooster|WD=Q2216236|lt=Rooster|1=Rooster}}; Zhēngyuè 2 is the day of the {{ill|Dog|en|Dog|WD=Q144|lt=Dog|1=Dog}}; Zhēngyuè 3 is the day of the {{ill|Pig|en|Pig|WD=Q787|lt=Pig|1=Pig}}; Zhēngyuè 4 is the day of the {{ill|Goat|en|Goat|WD=Q2934|lt=Goat|1=Goat}}; Zhēngyuè 5 is the day of the {{ill|Ox|en|Ox|WD=Q473194|lt=Ox|1=Ox}}; Zhēngyuè 6 is the day of the {{ill|Horse|en|Horse|WD=Q726|lt=Horse|1=Horse}}; Zhēngyuè 7 is the day of the {{ill|Human|en|Human|WD=Q5|lt=Human|1=Human}}.
'''Lunisolar January''' (旧暦一月) is the first {{ill|Month|en|Month|WD=Q5151|lt=Month|1=Month}} of the year in the {{ill|Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|ja|旧暦|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|11=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|qq=}} ({{ill|Lunisolar calendar|en|Lunisolar calendar|WD=Q194235|lt=Lunisolar calendar|1=Lunisolar calendar}}).
Under definitions predating the {{ill|Tenpō calendar|en|Tenpō calendar|WD=Q1066717|lt=Tenpō Calendar|1=Tenpō calendar}}, January was defined as the month that includes {{ill|Yushui (solar term)|en|Yushui (solar term)|WD=Q1364713|lt=Yushui|1=Yushui (solar term)}}. In the {{ill|New Calendar|ja|新暦|1=The new calendar|lt=New Calendar|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/New Calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/New Calendar (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q125314718}}, it corresponds approximately to late {{ill|January|en|January|WD=Q108|lt=January|1=January}} to early {{ill|March|en|March|WD=Q110|lt=March|1=March}}.
Another name for January is '''Mutsuki''' (睦月). The etymology is explained at [[#Etymology of traditional Japanese name]]
In the Eastern lunisolar calendar, the number of days in a month, known as {{ill|Large and Small|ja|大小|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Large and Small|1=Large and Small|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10935019}}, (with large months having 30 days and small months 29 days) varies from year to year. As a result, there are years when January ends on the 29th, with no January 30th.
==Etymology of traditional Japanese name==
In {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}, the '''Lunar January''' is called '''Mutsuki''' (むつき), and today it is also used as an alternate name for January of the {{ill|Gregorian calendar|ja|新暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gregorian calendar|1=The new calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q125314718}}. There are various theories about the origin of the name Mutsuki. The most widely accepted theory holds that it derives from “Mutsubizuki” (睦び月), meaning “month of harmony,” when all relatives gather to hold banquets. Other theories include “Mototsuki” (元つ月), “Moyutsuki” (萌月), and “Umutsuki” (生月).
In the Saru dialect of the {{ill|Ainu language|en|Ainu language|WD=Q27969|lt=Hokkaido Ainu language|1=Ainu language}}, it is called “Towetanne” (トウエタンネ; {{ill|Ainu language|en|Ainu language|WD=Q27969|lt=Ainu language|1=Ainu language}}: towetanne), meaning “the days become longer” (<ref>{{Cite web |title=アイヌ語カレンダー |url=https://www.ff-ainu.or.jp/calendardownload.html |website=(公益財団法人 アイヌ民族文化財団) |access-date=2025-01-12}}</ref>), and in the Asahikawa dialect, January is called “Inomi Cup” (イノミチュㇷ゚; {{ill|Ainu language|en|Ainu language|WD=Q27969|lt=Ainu language|1=Ainu language}}: inomi cup), meaning “month of praying to the gods” (<ref>{{Cite web |title=月刊シロㇿ 10月号（2015.10） |url=https://ainugo.nam.go.jp/siror/monthly/201510.html#01 |website=アイヌと自然 デジタル図鑑 |access-date=2025-01-09 |publisher={{ill|Ainu Museum|en|Ainu Museum|WD=Q10333378|lt=Ainu Museum|1=Ainu Museum}}}}</ref>). == Dates in Mutsuki == {| class="wikitable" |+ Dates in Mutsuki ! the late [[:en:Qing Empire|Qing Empire]], Zhēngyuè 5, 1823 *[[:en:Aisin-Gioro Puyi|Aisin-Gioro Puyi]], the last emperor of China and the twelfth and final ruler of the {{ill|Qing dynasty|en|Qing dynasty|WD=Q8733|lt=Qing dynasty|1=Qing dynasty}}, Zhēngyuè 14, 1906
==Death== *{{ill|Sun Jian|en|Sun Jian|WD=Q334101|lt=Sun Jian|1=Sun Jian}}, a military general and {{ill|Warlord|en|Warlord|WD=Q220098|lt=Warlord|1=Warlord}} who lived in the late [[:en:Eastern Han dynasty|Eastern Han dynasty]], Zhēngyuè 7, 191 *{{ill|Guan Yu|en|Guan Yu|WD=Q222797|lt=Guan Yu|1=Guan Yu}}, a general serving under the warlord {{ill|Liu Bei|en|Liu Bei|WD=Q245315|lt=Liu Bei|1=Liu Bei}} in the late Eastern Han dynasty, Zhēngyuè 13, 220 *{{ill|Cao Cao|en|Cao Cao|WD=Q204077|lt=Cao Cao|1=Cao Cao}}, a warlord and the penultimate Tang|en|Emperor Xianzong of Tang|WD=Q9761|lt=Emperor Xianzong of Tang|1=Emperor Xianzong of Tang}}, an emperor of the Chinese Tang dynasty, Zhēngyuè 27, 820 *{{ill|Emperor Yingzong of Song|en|Emperor Yingzong of Song|WD=Q7479|lt=Emperor Yingzong of Song|1=Emperor Yingzong of Song}}, the fifth emperor of the {{ill|Song dynasty|en|Song dynasty|WD=Q7462|lt=Song dynasty|1=Song dynasty}} of China, Empire}}), {{ill|reign|en|reign|WD=Q7310094|lt=reign|1=Reign}}ing from 1260 to 1294, and the founder of the {{ill|Yuan dynasty|en|Yuan dynasty|WD=Q7313|lt=Yuan dynasty|1=Yuan dynasty}} in {{ill|Mongolia|en|Mongolia|WD=Q711|lt=Mongolia|1=Mongolia}} and China, Zhēngyuè 22, 1294 *{{ill|Shunzhi Emperor|en|Shunzhi Emperor|WD=Q310453|lt=Shunzhi Emperor|1=Shunzhi Emperor}}, the third emperor of the Qing dynasty and the first Qing emperor to rule over China, from 1644 to 1661, Zhēngyuè 30, 1638 *{{ill|Qianlong Emperor|en|Qianlong Emperor|WD=Q19133|lt=Qianlong Emperor|1=Qianlong Emperor}}, the sixth emperor of the [[:en:Manchu|Manchu]]-led Qing dynasty, and the fourth Qing emperor to rule over China proper, Zhēngyuè 3, 1799 *{{ill|Daoguang Emperor|en|Daoguang Emperor|WD=Q334351|lt=Daoguang Emperor|1=Daoguang Emperor}}, a Qing emperor, Zhēngyuè 14, 1850

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[cdo:Nùng-lĭk 1 nguŏk]][[en:Zhengyue]][[gan:正月]][[hak:Nùng-la̍k 1-ngie̍t]][[ja:1月 (旧暦)]][[ko:음력 1월]][[th:เดือนอ้าย]][[vi:Tháng giêng]][[wuu:正月]][[za:Ndwencieng]][[zh-min-nan:Kū-le̍k 1 goe̍h]][[zh-yue:正月]][[zh:正月]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[cdo:Nùng-lĭk 1 nguŏk]][[en:Zhengyue]][[gan:正月]][[hak:Nùng-la̍k 1-ngie̍t]][[ja:1月 (旧暦)]][[ko:음력 1월]][[th:เดือนอ้าย]][[vi:Tháng giêng]][[wuu:正月]][[za:Ndwencieng]][[zh-min-nan:Kū-le̍k 1 goe̍h]][[zh-yue:正月]][[zh:正月]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[cdo:Nùng-lĭk 1 nguŏk]][[en:Zhengyue]][[gan:正月]][[hak:Nùng-la̍k 1-ngie̍t]][[ja:1月 (旧暦)]][[ko:음력 1월]][[th:เดือนอ้าย]][[vi:Tháng giêng]][[wuu:正月]][[za:Ndwencieng]][[zh-min-nan:Kū-le̍k 1 goe̍h]][[zh-yue:正月]][[zh:正月]]
{{Lunisolar January|qq=}} ''''Zhēngyuè''''' is the first {{ill|Month|en|Month|WD=Q5151|lt=Month|1=Month}} of the {{ill|Year|en|Year|WD=Q577|lt=Year|1=Year}} in the {{ill|Chinese calendar|en|Chinese calendar|WD=Q134032|lt=Chinese calendar|1=Chinese calendar}}. In general, the Vernal Showers are in Zhēngyuè. The name comes from the annual {{ill|Morale|en|Morale|WD=Q1465318|lt=Morale|1=Morale}}-building [[:en:Li (Confucian)|rite]] ({{zh|c=正旦大朝|qq=}}) in {{ill|Ancient China|en|Ancient China|WD=Q630276|lt=Ancient China|1=Ancient China}}.
Zhēngyuè 1 is the {{ill|Day|en|Day|WD=Q573|lt=Day|1=Day}} of the {{ill|Rooster|en|Rooster|WD=Q2216236|lt=Rooster|1=Rooster}}; Zhēngyuè 2 is the day of the {{ill|Dog|en|Dog|WD=Q144|lt=Dog|1=Dog}}; Zhēngyuè 3 is the day of the {{ill|Pig|en|Pig|WD=Q787|lt=Pig|1=Pig}}; Zhēngyuè 4 is the day of the {{ill|Goat|en|Goat|WD=Q2934|lt=Goat|1=Goat}}; Zhēngyuè 5 is the day of the {{ill|Ox|en|Ox|WD=Q473194|lt=Ox|1=Ox}}; Zhēngyuè 6 is the day of the {{ill|Horse|en|Horse|WD=Q726|lt=Horse|1=Horse}}; Zhēngyuè 7 is the day of the {{ill|Human|en|Human|WD=Q5|lt=Human|1=Human}}.
'''Lunisolar January''' (旧暦一月) is the first {{ill|Month|en|Month|WD=Q5151|lt=Month|1=Month}} of the year in the {{ill|Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|ja|旧暦|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|11=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|qq=}} ({{ill|Lunisolar calendar|en|Lunisolar calendar|WD=Q194235|lt=Lunisolar calendar|1=Lunisolar calendar}}).
Under definitions predating the {{ill|Tenpō calendar|en|Tenpō calendar|WD=Q1066717|lt=Tenpō Calendar|1=Tenpō calendar}}, January was defined as the month that includes {{ill|Yushui (solar term)|en|Yushui (solar term)|WD=Q1364713|lt=Yushui|1=Yushui (solar term)}}. In the {{ill|New Calendar|ja|新暦|1=The new calendar|lt=New Calendar|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/New Calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/New Calendar (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q125314718}}, it corresponds approximately to late {{ill|January|en|January|WD=Q108|lt=January|1=January}} to early {{ill|March|en|March|WD=Q110|lt=March|1=March}}.
Another name for January is '''Mutsuki''' (睦月). The etymology is explained at [[#Etymology of traditional Japanese name]]
In the Eastern lunisolar calendar, the number of days in a month, known as {{ill|Large and Small|ja|大小|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Large and Small|1=Large and Small|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10935019}}, (with large months having 30 days and small months 29 days) varies from year to year. As a result, there are years when January ends on the 29th, with no January 30th.
==Etymology of traditional Japanese name==
In {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}, the '''Lunar January''' is called '''Mutsuki''' (むつき), and today it is also used as an alternate name for January of the {{ill|Gregorian calendar|ja|新暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gregorian calendar|1=The new calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q125314718}}. There are various theories about the origin of the name Mutsuki. The most widely accepted theory holds that it derives from “Mutsubizuki” (睦び月), meaning “month of harmony,” when all relatives gather to hold banquets. Other theories include “Mototsuki” (元つ月), “Moyutsuki” (萌月), and “Umutsuki” (生月).
In the Saru dialect of the {{ill|Ainu language|en|Ainu language|WD=Q27969|lt=Hokkaido Ainu language|1=Ainu language}}, it is called “Towetanne” (トウエタンネ; {{ill|Ainu language|en|Ainu language|WD=Q27969|lt=Ainu language|1=Ainu language}}: towetanne), meaning “the days become longer” (<ref>{{Cite web |title=アイヌ語カレンダー |url=https://www.ff-ainu.or.jp/calendardownload.html |website=(公益財団法人 アイヌ民族文化財団) |access-date=2025-01-12}}</ref>), and in the Asahikawa dialect, January is called “Inomi Cup” (イノミチュㇷ゚; {{ill|Ainu language|en|Ainu language|WD=Q27969|lt=Ainu language|1=Ainu language}}: inomi cup), meaning “month of praying to the gods” (<ref>{{Cite web |title=月刊シロㇿ 10月号（2015.10） |url=https://ainugo.nam.go.jp/siror/monthly/201510.html#01 |website=アイヌと自然 デジタル図鑑 |access-date=2025-01-09 |publisher={{ill|Ainu Museum|en|Ainu Museum|WD=Q10333378|lt=Ainu Museum|1=Ainu Museum}}}}</ref>). == Dates in Mutsuki == {| class="wikitable" |+ Dates in Mutsuki ! Year !! New Moon !! Last Day !! Number of Days |- | 2014 || {{ill|January 31|en|January 31|WD=Q2289|lt=January 31|1=January 31}} || {{ill|February 28|en|February 28|WD=Q2362|lt=February 28|1=February 28}} || 29 days |- | 2015 || {{ill|February 19|en|February 19|WD=Q2340|lt=February 19|1=February 19}} || {{ill|March 19|en|March 19|WD=Q2424|lt=March 19|1=March 19}} || 29 days |- | 2016 || {{ill|February 8|en|February 8|WD=Q2323|lt=February 8|1=February 8}} || {{ill|March 8|en|March 8|WD=Q2395|lt=March 8|1=March 8}} || 30 days |- | 2017 || {{ill|January 28|en|January 28|WD=Q2285|lt=January 28|1=January 28}} || {{ill|February 25|en|February 25|WD=Q2356|lt=February 25|1=February 25}} || 29 days |- | 2018 || {{ill|February 16|en|February 16|WD=Q2342|lt=February 16|1=February 16}} || {{ill|March 16|en|March 16|WD=Q2417|lt=March 16|1=March 16}} || 29 days |- | 2019 || {{ill|February 5|en|February 5|WD=Q2319|lt=February 5|1=February 5}} || {{ill|March 6|en|March 6|WD=Q2399|lt=March 6|1=March 6}} || 30 days |- | 2020 || {{ill|January 25|en|January 25|WD=Q2279|lt=January 25|1=January 25}} || {{ill|February 23|en|February 23|WD=Q2352|lt=February 23|1=February 23}} || 30 days |- | 2021 || {{ill|February 12|en|February 12|WD=Q2336|lt=February 12|1=February 12}} || {{ill|March 12|en|March 12|WD=Q2402|lt=March 12|1=March 12}} || 29 days |- | 2022 || {{ill|February 1|en|February 1|WD=Q2312|lt=February 1|1=February 1}} || {{ill|March 2|en|March 2|WD=Q2390|lt=March 2|1=March 2}} || 30 days |- | 2023 || {{ill|January 22|en|January 22|WD=Q2275|lt=January 22|1=January 22}} || {{ill|February 19|en|February 19|WD=Q2340|lt=February 19|1=February 19}} || 29 days |- | 2024 || {{ill|February 10|en|February 10|WD=Q2325|lt=February 10|1=February 10}} || {{ill|March 9|en|March 9|WD=Q2401|lt=March 9|1=March 9}} || 29 days |- | 2025 || {{ill|January 29|en|January 29|WD=Q2286|lt=January 29|1=January 29}} || {{ill|February 27|en|February 27|WD=Q2361|lt=February 27|1=February 27}} || 30 days |}
== Overview == {{unreferenced|date=January 2015|qq=}} {{Italic title|reason=|qq=}}
==Festivals== *{{ill|Chinese New Year|en|Chinese New Year|WD=Q131772|lt=New Year's Day|1=Chinese New Year}} ({{zh|c=春节|qq=}}) is Zhēngyuè 1. The following New Year's Day is January 29, 2025, which is a statutory [[holiday]]. *The [[:en:Yuanxiao Festival|Yuanxiao Festival]] ({{zh|c=元宵|qq=}}) is Zhēngyuè 15. The following Yuanxiao is February 12, 2025.
==Birth== *{{ill|Wu Zetian|en|Wu Zetian|WD=Q9738|lt=Wu Zetian|1=Wu Zetian}}, the only {{ill|Female|en|Female|WD=Q43445|lt=Female|1=Female}} {{ill|Emperor|en|Emperor|WD=Q39018|lt=Emperor|1=Emperor}} of {{ill|China|en|China|WD=Q148|lt=China|1=China}}, Zhēngyuè 23, 624 *{{ill|en:Li Bai|en|en:Li Bai|WD=Q7071|lt=en:Li Bai|1=Li Bai}}, a Chinese {{ill|Poet|en|Poet|WD=Q49757|lt=Poet|1=Poet}} acclaimed to this day as a {{ill|genius|en|genius|WD=Q185030|lt=genius|1=Genius}} and romantic figure who took traditional {{ill|Poetry|en|Poetry|WD=Q482|lt=poetic|1=Poetry}} forms to new heights, Zhēngyuè 16, 701 *{{ill|Emperor Shunzong of Tang|en|Emperor Shunzong of Tang|WD=Q9760|lt=Emperor Shunzong of Tang|1=Emperor Shunzong of Tang}}, an emperor of the Chinese {{ill|Tang dynasty|en|Tang dynasty|WD=Q9683|lt=Tang dynasty|1=Tang dynasty}}, Zhēngyuè 12, 761 *{{ill|Bai Juyi|en|Bai Juyi|WD=Q313324|lt=Bai Juyi|1=Bai Juyi}}, a Chinese {{ill|Poet|en|Poet|WD=Q49757|lt=Poet|1=Poet}} of the Tang dynasty, Zhēngyuè 20, 772 *{{ill|Emperor Xizong of Jin|en|Emperor Xizong of Jin|WD=Q5071|lt=Emperor Xizong of Jin|1=Emperor Xizong of Jin}}, an emperor of the {{ill|Jin dynasty (1115–1234)|en|Jin dynasty (1115–1234)|WD=Q5066|lt=Jin dynasty|1=Jin dynasty (1115–1234)}} which controlled northern China from 1115 to 1234, Zhēngyuè 17, 1119 *{{ill|Qiu Chuji|en|Qiu Chuji|WD=Q565148|lt=Qiu Chuji|1=Qiu Chuji}}, a [[:en:Daoist|Daoist]] of {{ill|Wang Chongyang|en|Wang Chongyang|WD=Q701186|lt=Wang Chongyang|1=Wang Chongyang}}, Zhēngyuè 9, 1148 *{{ill|Itō Suketaka|en|Itō Suketaka|WD=Q8014919|lt=Itō Suketaka|1=Itō Suketaka}}, a samurai, daimyō and twelfth family leader of the Itō clan, Zhēngyuè 15, 1559 *{{ill|Li Hongzhang|en|Li Hongzhang|WD=Q378517|lt=Li Hongzhang|1=Li Hongzhang}}, a {{ill|Politician|en|Politician|WD=Q82955|lt=Politician|1=Politician}}, {{ill|General|en|General|WD=Q83460|lt=General|1=General}}, and {{ill|Diplomat|en|Diplomat|WD=Q193391|lt=Diplomat|1=Diplomat}} of the late [[:en:Qing Empire|Qing Empire]], Zhēngyuè 5, 1823 *[[:en:Aisin-Gioro Puyi|Aisin-Gioro Puyi]], the last emperor of China and the twelfth and final ruler of the {{ill|Qing dynasty|en|Qing dynasty|WD=Q8733|lt=Qing dynasty|1=Qing dynasty}}, Zhēngyuè 14, 1906

revision.text:
{{Lunisolar January|qq=}} ''''Zhēngyuè''''' is the first {{ill|Month|en|Month|WD=Q5151|lt=Month|1=Month}} of the {{ill|Year|en|Year|WD=Q577|lt=Year|1=Year}} in the {{ill|Chinese calendar|en|Chinese calendar|WD=Q134032|lt=Chinese calendar|1=Chinese calendar}}. In general, the Vernal Showers are in Zhēngyuè. The name comes from the annual {{ill|Morale|en|Morale|WD=Q1465318|lt=Morale|1=Morale}}-building [[:en:Li (Confucian)|rite]] ({{zh|c=正旦大朝|qq=}}) in {{ill|Ancient China|en|Ancient China|WD=Q630276|lt=Ancient China|1=Ancient China}}.
Zhēngyuè 1 is the {{ill|Day|en|Day|WD=Q573|lt=Day|1=Day}} of the {{ill|Rooster|en|Rooster|WD=Q2216236|lt=Rooster|1=Rooster}}; Zhēngyuè 2 is the day of the {{ill|Dog|en|Dog|WD=Q144|lt=Dog|1=Dog}}; Zhēngyuè 3 is the day of the {{ill|Pig|en|Pig|WD=Q787|lt=Pig|1=Pig}}; Zhēngyuè 4 is the day of the {{ill|Goat|en|Goat|WD=Q2934|lt=Goat|1=Goat}}; Zhēngyuè 5 is the day of the {{ill|Ox|en|Ox|WD=Q473194|lt=Ox|1=Ox}}; Zhēngyuè 6 is the day of the {{ill|Horse|en|Horse|WD=Q726|lt=Horse|1=Horse}}; Zhēngyuè 7 is the day of the {{ill|Human|en|Human|WD=Q5|lt=Human|1=Human}}.
'''Lunisolar January''' (旧暦一月) is the first {{ill|Month|en|Month|WD=Q5151|lt=Month|1=Month}} of the year in the {{ill|Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|ja|旧暦|1=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|11=Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese Lunisolar Calendar|qq=}} ({{ill|Lunisolar calendar|en|Lunisolar calendar|WD=Q194235|lt=Lunisolar calendar|1=Lunisolar calendar}}).
Under definitions predating the {{ill|Tenpō calendar|en|Tenpō calendar|WD=Q1066717|lt=Tenpō Calendar|1=Tenpō calendar}}, January was defined as the month that includes {{ill|Yushui (solar term)|en|Yushui (solar term)|WD=Q1364713|lt=Yushui|1=Yushui (solar term)}}. In the {{ill|New Calendar|ja|新暦|1=The new calendar|lt=New Calendar|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/New Calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/New Calendar (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q125314718}}, it corresponds approximately to late {{ill|January|en|January|WD=Q108|lt=January|1=January}} to early {{ill|March|en|March|WD=Q110|lt=March|1=March}}.
Another name for January is '''Mutsuki''' (睦月). The etymology is explained at [[#Etymology of traditional Japanese name]]
In the Eastern lunisolar calendar, the number of days in a month, known as {{ill|Large and Small|ja|大小|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Large and Small|1=Large and Small|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10935019}}, (with large months having 30 days and small months 29 days) varies from year to year. As a result, there are years when January ends on the 29th, with no January 30th.
==Etymology of traditional Japanese name==
In {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}, the '''Lunar January''' is called '''Mutsuki''' (むつき), and today it is also used as an alternate name for January of the {{ill|Gregorian calendar|ja|新暦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gregorian calendar|1=The new calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q125314718}}. There are various theories about the origin of the name Mutsuki. The most widely accepted theory holds that it derives from “Mutsubizuki” (睦び月), meaning “month of harmony,” when all relatives gather to hold banquets. Other theories include “Mototsuki” (元つ月), “Moyutsuki” (萌月), and “Umutsuki” (生月).
In the Saru dialect of the {{ill|Ainu language|en|Ainu language|WD=Q27969|lt=Hokkaido Ainu language|1=Ainu language}}, it is called “Towetanne” (トウエタンネ; {{ill|Ainu language|en|Ainu language|WD=Q27969|lt=Ainu language|1=Ainu language}}: towetanne), meaning “the days become longer” (<ref>{{Cite web |title=アイヌ語カレンダー |url=https://www.ff-ainu.or.jp/calendardownload.html |website=(公益財団法人 アイヌ民族文化財団) |access-date=2025-01-12}}</ref>), and in the Asahikawa dialect, January is called “Inomi Cup” (イノミチュㇷ゚; {{ill|Ainu language|en|Ainu language|WD=Q27969|lt=Ainu language|1=Ainu language}}: inomi cup), meaning “month of praying to the gods” (<ref>{{Cite web |title=月刊シロㇿ 10月号（2015.10） |url=https://ainugo.nam.go.jp/siror/monthly/201510.html#01 |website=アイヌと自然 デジタル図鑑 |access-date=2025-01-09 |publisher={{ill|Ainu Museum|en|Ainu Museum|WD=Q10333378|lt=Ainu Museum|1=Ainu Museum}}}}</ref>). == Dates in Mutsuki == {| class="wikitable" |+ Dates in Mutsuki ! == Overview == {{unreferenced|date=January 2015|qq=}} {{Italic title|reason=|qq=}}
==Festivals== *{{ill|Chinese New Year|en|Chinese New Year|WD=Q131772|lt=New Year's Day|1=Chinese New Year}} ({{zh|c=春节|qq=}}) is Zhēngyuè 1. The following New Year's Day is January 29, 2025, which is a statutory [[holiday]]. *The [[:en:Yuanxiao Festival|Yuanxiao Festival]] ({{zh|c=元宵|qq=}}) is Zhēngyuè 15. The following Yuanxiao is February 12, 2025.
